解释一下下列python代码的作用class LSTMValidationModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, configs):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(input_size=configs.input_size, hidden_size=configs.hidden_size, num_layers=configs.num_layers, bidirectional=configs.bidirectional, batch_first=True)
        self.linear = nn.Linear(configs.hidden_size, configs.output_size)

    def forward(self, x):
        x, _ = self.lstm(x)
        x = x[:, -1, :]
        x = self.linear(x)
        return x

Find various metaphors in this text "‘Devastating’ outcomes for woman abused by police officer husband after Ibac failures, report finds

Woman’s escape plan had previously been leaked to abuser, but Victorian anti-corruption body referred case back to police’s internal investigations.

Victoria’s anti-corruption commission fundamentally failed a woman who was abused by her police officer spouse, with delays in the handling of her case meaning a second officer who leaked her escape plan could not be prosecuted, a report has found.

The Victorian Inspectorate, the body responsible for overseeing the Independent Broad-based Anti-corruption Commission (Ibac), tabled a special report to parliament on Tuesday regarding the case of a woman known as Emma.

It said Ibac only appeared to take Emma’s case seriously after she wrote directly to the Ibac commissioner and told him she had taken her concerns to the media.

But in an extraordinary response, Ibac said that while it did not want to detract from Emma’s story, it had considered taking legal action against the inspectorate in relation to the report because of “jurisdictional error”, only deciding against doing so because it considered it would “not be in the public interest for two of Victoria’s major integrity institutions to be involved in litigation … at the public’s expense.”

Police first became aware that Emma was being abused by her former husband, senior constable Darren Hanegraaf, in 2018. Hanegraaf pleaded guilty to family violence offences in February 2020.

Emma had serious concerns about Victoria police’s handling of her case, including that confidential information she had provided to a family violence officer about a plan for her and her children to escape her husband interstate had been given to her abuser.

She made a complaint to Ibac, the body tasked with police oversight in Victoria, in November 2018. A second complaint was also made to Ibac about Victoria police about two years later.

Rather than investigate, Ibac referred both of these complaints back to Victoria police’s professional standards command, which handles the force’s internal investigations.

The inspectorate found Ibac’s referrals had serious consequences, including negative impacts on Emma and her children’s welfare, and meant she did not receive a better police response in the context of serious and ongoing family violence.

It also found that the referrals meant that the possibility of corruption was not pursued; that serious conflicts of interest at Victoria police were allowed to affect the independent investigation process; and that the alleged unwillingness of police to act against their own was effectively overlooked.

“The VI views these consequences as a fundamental failing of the integrity system,” the report found.

“The issues with Ibac’s handling of Emma’s case are so serious and long running, and the consequences so devastating, that it is worthy of consideration and public comment.”

Emma, who has previously used other pseudonyms including Michelle and Jay when detailing her experiences in the media, told Guardian Australia that she was dismayed by Ibac’s response to her complaint, and the fact it had still failed to apologise.

The lawyer with more than two decades’ experience has started a support group for the partners of abusive police officers.

“Ibac has shown a complete lack of insight, remorse and empathy in effectively saying they would do the same thing again if my case came across them again, despite the catastrophic outcome for me and my children.

“We’ve suffered an immense amount because of Ibac’s failures, and we won’t ever get justice or accountability, but at least getting … an acknowledgment of a screw up is a start.

“We’ve got prima facie evidence of an integrity body with an integrity problem. This is absolutely not about resourcing – this is about mismanagement and culture.”

Ibac’s deputy commissioner, Kylie Kilgour, said in a statement that without government reform to the commission’s jurisdiction and funding it had “little choice but to refer matters such as Emma’s to Victoria Police”.

In a 27-page response to the inspectorate which was included in the report, Ibac details a litany of failures it said the inspectorate made, accuses them of bias, and makes clear it considered taking them to the supreme court.

But it ultimately accepted four recommendations, including that it keeps better records, develops policy regarding complaints it refers on and the ways in which these referrals can be withdrawn, and ensures the inspectorate is informed by Ibac as soon as possible about complaints that have been made against them."

Summarize the following article What comes to mind when you hear the word “robot”? Do you picture a metallic humanoid in a spaceship in the distant future? Perhaps you imagine a dystopian future where humanity is enslaved by its robot overlords. Or maybe you think of an automobile assembly line with robot-like machines putting cars together.
Whatever you think, one thing is sure: robots are here to stay. Fortunately, it seems likely that robots will be more about doing repetitive or dangerous tasks than seizing supreme executive power. Let’s look at robotics, defining and classifying the term, figuring out the role of Artificial Intelligence in the field, the future of robotics, and how robotics will change our lives.

What Is Robotics?
Robotics is the engineering branch that deals with the conception, design, construction, operation, application, and usage of robots. Digging a little deeper, we see that robots are defined as an automatically operated machine that carries out a series of actions independently and does the work usually accomplished by a human.
Incidentally, robots don’t have to resemble humans, although some do. Look at images of automobile assembly lines for proof. Robots that appear human are typically referred to as “androids.” Although robot designers make their creations appear human so that people feel more at ease around them, it’s not always the case. Some people find robots, especially ones that resemble people, creepy.
Types of Robots
Robots are versatile machines, evidenced by their wide variety of forms and functions. Here's a list of a few kinds of robots we see today:
Healthcare: Robots in the healthcare industry do everything from assisting in surgery to physical therapy to help people walk to moving through hospitals and delivering essential supplies such as meds or linens. Healthcare robots have even contributed to the ongoing fight against the pandemic, filling and sealing testing swabs and producing respirators.
Homelife: You need look no further than a Roomba to find a robot in someone's house. But they do more now than vacuuming floors; home-based robots can mow lawns or augment tools like Alexa.
Manufacturing: The field of manufacturing was the first to adopt robots, such as the automobile assembly line machines we previously mentioned. Industrial robots handle a various tasks like arc welding, material handling, steel cutting, and food packaging.
Logistics: Everybody wants their online orders delivered on time, if not sooner. So companies employ robots to stack warehouse shelves, retrieve goods, and even conduct short-range deliveries.
Space Exploration: Mars explorers such as Sojourner and Perseverance are robots. The Hubble telescope is classified as a robot, as are deep space probes like Voyager and Cassini.
Military: Robots handle dangerous tasks, and it doesn't get any more difficult than modern warfare. Consequently, the military enjoys a diverse selection of robots equipped to address many of the riskier jobs associated with war. For example, there's the Centaur, an explosive detection/disposal robot that looks for mines and IEDs, the MUTT, which follows soldiers around and totes their gear, and SAFFiR, which fights fires that break out on naval vessels.
Entertainment: We already have toy robots, robot statues, and robot restaurants. As robots become more sophisticated, expect their entertainment value to rise accordingly.
Travel: We only need to say three words: self-driving vehicles.
Explore Career Opportunities in AI & ML

Advantages and Disadvantages of Robots
Like any innovation today, robots have their plusses and negatives. Here’s a breakdown of the good and bad about robots and the future of robotics.
Advantages
They work in hazardous environments: Why risk human lives when you can send a robot in to do the job? Consider how preferable it is to have a robot fighting a fire or working on a nuclear reactor core.
They’re cost-effective: Robots don’t take sick days or coffee breaks, nor need perks like life insurance, paid time off, or healthcare offerings like dental and vision.
They increase productivity: Robots are wired to perform repetitive tasks ad infinitum; the human brain is not. Industries use robots to accomplish the tedious, redundant work, freeing employees to tackle more challenging tasks and even learn new skills.
They offer better quality assurance: Vigilance decrement is a lapse in concentration that hits workers who repeatedly perform the same functions. As the human’s concentration level drops, the likelihood of errors, poor results, or even accidents increases. Robots perform repetitive tasks flawlessly without having their performance slip due to boredom.
Disadvantages
They incur deep startup costs: Robot implementation is an investment risk, and it costs a lot. Although most manufacturers eventually see a recoup of their investment over the long run, it's expensive in the short term. However, this is a common obstacle in new technological implementation, like setting up a wireless network or performing cloud migration.
They might take away jobs: Yes, some people have been replaced by robots in certain situations, like assembly lines, for instance. Whenever the business sector incorporates game-changing technology, some jobs become casualties. However, this disadvantage might be overstated because robot implementation typically creates a greater demand for people to support the technology, which brings up the final disadvantage.
They require companies to hire skilled support staff: This drawback is good news for potential employees, but bad news for thrifty-minded companies. Robots require programmers, operators, and repair personnel. While job seekers may rejoice, the prospect of having to recruit professionals (and pay professional-level salaries!) may serve as an impediment to implementing robots.
The Future of Robotics: What’s the Use of AI in Robotics?
Artificial Intelligence (AI) increases human-robot interaction, collaboration opportunities, and quality. The industrial sector already has co-bots, which are robots that work alongside humans to perform testing and assembly.
Advances in AI help robots mimic human behavior more closely, which is why they were created in the first place. Robots that act and think more like people can integrate better into the workforce and bring a level of efficiency unmatched by human employees.
Robot designers use Artificial Intelligence to give their creations enhanced capabilities like:
Computer Vision: Robots can identify and recognize objects they meet, discern details, and learn how to navigate or avoid specific items.
Manipulation: AI helps robots gain the fine motor skills needed to grasp objects without destroying the item.
Motion Control and Navigation: Robots no longer need humans to guide them along paths and process flows. AI enables robots to analyze their environment and self-navigate. This capability even applies to the virtual world of software. AI helps robot software processes avoid flow bottlenecks or process exceptions.
Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Real-World Perception: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (ML) help robots better understand their surroundings, recognize and identify patterns, and comprehend data. These improvements increase the robot’s autonomy and decrease reliance on human agents.

A Word About Robot Software
Software robots are computer programs that perform tasks without human intervention, such as web crawlers or chatbots. These robots are entirely virtual and not considered actual robots since they have no physical characteristics.
This technology shouldn't be confused with robotic software loaded into a robot and determines its programming. However, it's normal to experience overlap between the two entities since, in both cases, the software is helping the entity (robot or computer program) perform its functions independent of human interaction.
The Future of Robotics and Robots
Thanks to improved sensor technology and more remarkable advances in Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence, robots will keep moving from mere rote machines to collaborators with cognitive functions. These advances, and other associated fields, are enjoying an upwards trajectory, and robotics will significantly benefit from these strides.
We can expect to see more significant numbers of increasingly sophisticated robots incorporated into more areas of life, working with humans. Contrary to dystopian-minded prophets of doom, these improved robots will not replace workers. Industries rise and fall, and some become obsolete in the face of new technologies, bringing new opportunities for employment and education.
That’s the case with robots. Perhaps there will be fewer human workers welding automobile frames, but there will be a greater need for skilled technicians to program, maintain, and repair the machines. In many cases, this means that employees could receive valuable in-house training and upskilling, giving them a set of skills that could apply to robot programming and maintenance and other fields and industries.
The Future of Robotics: How Robots Will Change the World
Robots will increase economic growth and productivity and create new career opportunities for many people worldwide. However, there are still warnings out there about massive job losses, forecasting losses of 20 million manufacturing jobs by 2030, or how 30% of all jobs could be automated by 2030.
But thanks to the consistent levels of precision that robots offer, we can look forward to robots handling more of the burdensome, redundant manual labor tasks, making transportation work more efficiently, improving healthcare, and freeing people to improve themselves. But, of course, time will tell how this all works out.

Short summary of Fluid HTN:
Fluid HTN, a simple HTN planner based on the Builder pattern, inspired by Fluid Behaviour Tree. HTN stands for Hierarchical Task Network, which is a method of AI planning that decomposes high-level tasks into low-level actions. The page explains the main concepts and features of Fluid HTN, such as:
Compound Tasks: high-level tasks that have multiple ways of being accomplished, such as Selectors and Sequencers.
Primitive Tasks: low-level tasks that represent a single step that can be performed by the AI, such as Operators, Effects and Conditions.
Effects: world state changes that can be applied during planning or execution, such as PlanOnly, PlanAndExecute and Permanent effects.
Domain Builder: a fluent API to simplify the design of code-oriented HTN domains, with support for custom conditions, effects, operators and selectors.
Partial planning: a feature that allows the planner to only plan up to a certain point, then continue from there once the partial plan has been completed, using Pause Plan tasks.
Domain splicing: a feature that allows the planner to splice together sub-domains to form new domains, using Splice tasks or Slot tasks for run-time splicing.
Factory interface: an interface that allows the user to customize memory management schemes, such as pooling, for arrays/collections/objects used by the planner.
Decomposition logging: a feature that allows the planner to store information about condition success and failure during decomposition, for debugging purposes.
Callback hooks: a feature that allows the user to hook up to certain events in the planner, such as OnNewPlan or OnCurrentTaskFailed.

According to Hesiod, the Giants are the offspring of Gaia, born from the drops of blood that fell on the ground when Cronus castrated his father Uranus;[75] there is, however, no mention of a battle between the gods and the Giants in the Theogony.[76] It is Apollodorus who provides the most complete account of the Gigantomachy. He says that Gaia, out of anger at how Zeus had imprisoned her children, the Titans, bore the Giants to Uranus.[77] There comes to the gods a prophecy that the Giants cannot be defeated by the gods on their own, but can be defeated only with the help of a mortal; Gaia, upon hearing of this, seeks a special pharmakon (herb) that will prevent the Giants from being killed. Zeus, however, orders Eos (Dawn), Selene (Moon) and Helios (Sun) to stop shining, and harvests all of the herb himself, before having Athena summon Heracles.[78] In the conflict, Porphyrion, one of the most powerful of the Giants, launches an attack upon Heracles and Hera; Zeus, however, causes Porphyrion to become lustful for Hera, and when he is just about to violate her, Zeus strikes him with his thunderbolt, before Heracles deals the fatal blow with an arrow.[79]

I believe it is a priceless evangelistic opportunity when people are in times of sorrow. People are bewildered by the loss of possessions, immediate family members, and loved ones, and the great frustration and grief that loss brings. On the other hand, Christians actually gain a great deal by losing. We have gained a new person by losing an old one. We have also put on righteousness by forsaking our sinful nature. Of course, we cannot be completely sinless, and we cannot live forever in this body, but we do have the promise of how we can live the eternity that follows after our time in the body has ended.
When I was leading a Japanese ministry in North Carolina, I officiated at as many as five funerals a week. One of them was for the son of a Buddhist woman. She told me, "My son was born and raised as an American citizen, so a Christian funeral would be better." For a moment I thought I would refuse the funeral. But then I thought about it and realized that there would be many members of the Buddhist congregation at the funeral. Buddhists or not, people without eternal promises would be at the funeral. It was filled with people who were grieving that the person who had died was no longer there, along with a Buddhist woman who had lost her son. In other words, they were grieving that there was no hope for him who had died.
To the congregation at such a funeral, I gave a straightforward message. "I had never spoken to him before his death, so I have no idea what exactly will happen to him after his physical death. But what I can tell those of you who are alive today is that we are left with the choice to live forever." And so I spoke from the Bible about salvation from sin and the promise of eternal life in Jesus Christ and I closed my message by saying, "If you are interested, please come to our church.” It is not often that one has the opportunity to speak such a message in front of dozens of Buddhists.
If you went out into the city today and hooked up a microphone to a karaoke machine and gave a Bible message in a loud voice, how many people would be silent and listen? They would probably say, "It's grating on my ears, stop it!" But what would you think if I told someone who has lost his home, family, job, precious memories, and hopes about a treasure that will never decay? If those who know about eternal life and the One who gives it to us do not speak, who can speak to such people? Now is not the time for us to be complacent in the comfort of our own churches. If the church is the body of Christ, shouldn't we start doing what Jesus Himself would do?

;;;;;;; --- Profile Your Functions --- ;;;;;;;;;
;;   by (C.) Vladimir Nesterovsky, December 1998.
;; <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>,  http://vnestr.tripod.com
;;   Free for *non-commercial* personal use only
;;         with this notice unchanged.
;;
;; Here's an AutoLISP profiler for measuring the execution
;; speed of your functions. Good for r12-r14; to use it under 
;; r2000, change all your DEFUN's to DEFUN-Q's. 
;; Unfortunately, we don't have access to a function's 
;; lambda-list anymore in r2000's VisualLISP. :-(
;;
;; Happy profiling!
;;
;;
;; usage: (profile-func 'your-function), then
;;        call it or a program that's calling it, 
;;        then use (show-timers).
;;        When finished, call (profile-stop).
;;
;; potential problems: 
;;  slows down the execution while profiling many functions;
;;  time is measured by (getvar "date") that might not have 
;;  fine enough resolution, depending on platform, 
;;  so timings for fast routines might be not so reliable.
;;  but you can measure the execution of many repetitions of the
;;  same code with (defun test()(repeat 10000 (yourfunction)))
;;  and profiling it instead, with (profile-func 'test).
;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;

;; global variables
;;  vpn__profiled_funcs *symbol-names*
;;  various "vpn__*" vars during the execution
(DEFUN PROFILE-FUNC( FSYM / f fname arg-list formals )
 (if (not (atom fsym))
  (mapcar 'profile-func fsym)
  (cond
    ((or (/= 'SYM (type FSYM))
          (not (ufuncp (SETQ F (EVAL FSYM))))
     )
     (mapcar 'princ '(
       " must be quoted symbol of"
       " user-defined function to be profiled. "))
     (princ)
     )
    ((member fsym vpn__profiled_funcs)
      (princ " ")
      (princ (sym2str fsym))
      (princ " is being profiled already. ")
      (princ)
     )
    (T
      ;; profile this function
      (setq fname (sym2str fsym))

      (IF (= 'PAGETB (TYPE(CAR F))) ;; r12 stuff :-)))
        (SETQ F (CDR F))
      )
      (SET (READ (STRCAT "vpn__orig_func_" FNAME)) F)

      (SETQ ARG-LIST (CAR F)
            FORMALS (if (MEMBER '/ ARG-LIST)
                      (REVERSE (CDR (MEMBER '/ 
                         (REVERSE ARG-LIST))))
                      arg-list
                    )
      )
      (princ
      (EVAL (LIST 'DEFUN Fsym
                    (append formals 
                      (list '/ 'result 'strtm 'endtm ))
        '(setq strtm (getvar "date"))
        (LIST 'SETQ 'RESULT
              (CONS (READ (STRCAT "vpn__orig_func_" FNAME)) 
                            FORMALS))
        '(setq endtm (getvar "date"))
        (list '+= (list 'quote 
               (READ (STRCAT "VPN__TIMER_FOR_" FNAME)))
            '(* 86400 (- endtm strtm))
        )
        (list '+= (list 'quote 
          (read (strcat "vpn__calls_for_" FNAME))) 1)
        'RESULT
      )))
      (setq vpn__profiled_funcs 
        (cons fsym vpn__profiled_funcs))
      (princ " ")
      (princ)
    )
  )
))

(defun profile-stop()
  (restore-funcs)
  (foreach x
    (atoms "vpn__timer_for_*,vpn__calls_for_*")
    (set (read x) nil)
  )
  (setq vpn__profiled_funcs nil)
)

(DEFUN RESTART-TIMERS()
  (foreach x (atoms "vpn__timer_for_*,vpn__calls_for_*")
    (set (read x) 0)
  )
)

(defun show-timers( / as nn tm )
 (cond
  ((setq as (atoms "vpn__timer_for_*"))
   (foreach x (acad_strlsort as)
    (princ "\n")
    (princ (substr x 6))(princ ": n=")
    (princ (setq nn (eval (read 
       (strcat "vpn__calls_for_" (substr x 16))))))
    (princ ", t=")
    (princ (setq tm (eval (read x))))
    (princ ", t/call=")(princ 
       (if (zerop nn) "N/A" (/ (float tm) nn)))
    (princ ".")
   ))
  ((princ "\n No timers are being maintained."))
  )
 (princ "\n")
 (princ)
)

(defun restore-funcs()
  (foreach x (atoms "vpn__orig_func_*")
    (set (read (substr x 16)) (eval (read x)))
    (set (read x) nil)
  )
)

(defun restore-func(s / q2)
  (if (strp s)
    (if (eval (setq q2 (read 
                (strcat "vpn__orig_func_" s))))
    (progn
      (set (read s) (eval q2))
      (set q2 nil)
      (setq vpn__profiled_funcs 
        (minuslist vpn__profiled_funcs (list (read s))))
      (foreach x
        (atoms (strcat "vpn__timer_for_" s 
                       ",vpn__calls_for_" s))
        (set (read x) nil)
      )
    ))
    (if s (restore-func (sym2str s)))
))

(defun show-funcs()
  (foreach x (atoms "vpn__orig_func_*")
   (print (list (setq x (substr x 16)) (eval(read x)))))
  (princ)
)

(defun sym2str (sym ) 
 (if (null *symbol-names*) ;; cache list empty
     (setq *symbol-names*  ;; build it
       (mapcar 'cons
         (atoms-family 0)
         (atoms-family 1))))
 (cond
   ((cdr(assoc sym *symbol-names*)))
   ((cdar    ;; update cache list using SYMSTR
    (setq *symbol-names*    
      (cons (cons sym (symstr sym))
            *symbol-names*)))) ))

;;convert symbol to string; found on Internet.
(defun symstr (sym) 
    (if (= (type sym) 'SYM)   
      ( (list
          (if (boundp sym)
            (cons '/ (atoms-family 0))
            (cons '/ (cons sym (atoms-family 0)))
          )
          (list setq sym 1)
          (list car (list atoms-family 1))
        ) ) ) )

(defun ufuncp(f)   ;; User FUNCtion Predicate
  (and
    f
    (listp f)
    (or
      (null (car f))              ;; no args
      (= (type (car f)) 'PAGETB)  ;; VMON
      (and                        ;; args list
        (listp (car f))
        (null (cdr (last* (car f))))   ;; not dotted list
        (apply '= (cons 'SYM 
          (mapcar 'type (car f)))) ) ) ) )

;; last cons cell of a list
(defun last* (lst)
  (while (not (atom (cdr lst)))
    (setq lst (cdr lst)))
  lst)

(defun += (symbolvariable incrementvalue)
 (if (numberp (eval symbolvariable))
   (set symbolvariable (+ incrementvalue
     (eval symbolvariable)))
   (set symbolvariable incrementvalue)
 ))

(defun atoms (pattern / lst)
 (setq pattern (strcase pattern))
 (foreach atomstring (atoms-family 1)
  (if (wcmatch atomstring pattern) 
    (setq lst (cons atomstring lst))))
 (reverse lst))

(defun minuslist ( fromlist elementslist / tmplst )
 (foreach element fromlist 
   (if (not (member element elementslist))
     (setq tmplst (cons element tmplst))))
 (reverse tmplst)
)

(defun profile-usage()
  (mapcar 'princ '(
    "\n       ---= Profile AutoLISP functions =---  "
    "\n by Vladimir Nesterovsky, <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>"
    " 1998.\n\nUse (PROFILE-FUNC 'fsym), (RESTORE-FUNCS),"
    " (SHOW-FUNCS), (SHOW-TIMERS), "
    "\n (RESTART-TIMERS), (PROFILE-USAGE) "
    "and (PROFILE-STOP) to stop profiling."
    ))
  (princ)
)
(profile-usage)
;;;;;;;;;; PROFILE ends ;;;;;;;;;;;

I arrived at Harajuku, which is called the fashion street of Tokyo. There were so many people wearing various kinds of costumes, even though it was not Halloween. I felt people's confidence walking around without being judged by others. I loved how the people respected each other's preferences. 
 
After walking around the city, we had a late dinner, which was Yakiniku. It is a beef or pork dish, and the word yaki means grilled. It was very delicious since I like all kinds of meat, so I especially liked this. After dinner, I went up to the Tokyo Tower in order to enjoy the nighttime sightseeing. As I looked at the fabulous city lights, I wrapped up the first day of travel with my mom.

While I was touring around, there were numerous people and various shops. I got so excited. I wore a gorgeous Yukata and wandered around Sensoji. Every single tourist was watching me because I might have been lovely.

Hello and welcome to our YouTube channel. In this video, we will talk about ocean acidification, an important environmental issue that is affecting our oceans. We’ll explain what ocean acidification is, why it’s happening, and what we can do to help. So, let’s dive in!
Section 1: Ocean Acidification - The Basics
Understanding ocean acidification is critical to addressing its impact on marine life. In this first section, we will explain what ocean acidification is, how it happens, and what it means for marine life. We will also highlight essential concepts like pH levels and carbon dioxide.

Ocean acidification is a process that occurs when carbon dioxide (CO2) from the atmosphere dissolves into the ocean, causing a decrease in pH levels. The ocean’s pH has been decreasing since the industrial age because carbon dioxide concentrations have been increasing.

(Source: https://www.epa.gov/ocean-acidification/what-ocean-acidification )

Carbon dioxide is released by human activities such as burning fossil fuels like coal, oil, and natural gas, which contribute to global warming and climate change, as well as ocean acidification. When CO2 is dissolved in the ocean, it reacts with water molecules to form carbonic acid, which results in the decrease in pH levels.

(Source: https://www.climate.gov/news-features/understanding-climate/climate-change-atmospheric-carbon-dioxide )


Ocean acidification has significant impacts on marine life, affecting everything from small plankton to large marine mammals. The decrease in pH levels can result in weaker shells for shell-forming organisms like oysters and clams and can also affect the ability of marine organisms to grow and reproduce. Moreover, ocean acidification often leads to coral bleaching, which results in the death of coral reefs, the most diverse marine ecosystem on earth.

(Source: https://www.nature.com/scitable/knowledge/library/ocean-acidification-and-its-potential-impact-on-24344790/ )


pH is a measure of acidity or alkalinity, ranging from 0 (most acidic) to 14 (most alkaline), with 7 being neutral. The pH of the ocean’s surface waters has decreased from 8.2 to 8.1 over the past century, and the rate of this decrease is accelerating.

(Source: https://scijinks.gov/ocean-acidification )

In conclusion, ocean acidification is a critical issue for marine life, caused by an increase in carbon dioxide emissions resulting in a decrease in pH levels. This process has severe consequences for marine organisms, such as weakened shells and coral bleaching, leading to damaging impacts on the entire marine ecosystem. Understanding the science behind ocean acidification is essential to mitigating its impact on the ocean, and ultimately our planet.


Section 2: Impacts of Ocean Acidification
The ocean is a diverse and complex ecosystem, and it is being threatened by a process known as ocean acidification. In this section, we’ll talk about the impact of ocean acidification on different marine organisms, including shellfish, coral reefs, and plankton. We’ll also discuss how it is affecting the food web, biodiversity, and ecosystem services.

For many shellfish, ocean acidification presents a serious threat. As the ocean becomes more acidic, it affects the ability of shell-forming organisms to produce and maintain their protective shells. This leads to weaker shells and increased susceptibility to predators and other threats.

(Source: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0048969719360877 )

Coral reefs are one of the most vibrant and diverse ecosystems on Earth, but they are also highly vulnerable to ocean acidification. As the pH levels of the ocean change, coral reefs become stressed and can begin to bleach, becoming less colorful and more vulnerable to disease and other threats.

(Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/nclimate2874 )


Plankton at the base of the ocean food web are also affected by ocean acidification. They are essential to the survival of many other marine organisms, and as the pH levels of the ocean change, they can be disrupted, leading to major changes in the food web and overall ecosystem health.

(Source: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/233516047_The_effects_of_ocean_acidification_on_phragmatopoma_californica )



Ocean acidification is not just affecting individual species, it is also affecting the entire marine ecosystem. As species are disrupted or disappear, this can have a ripple effect on other species, leading to major changes in biodiversity and ultimately affecting the ecosystem services we rely on, such as food production and climate regulation.

(Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5958912/ )

In conclusion, the effects of ocean acidification are complex and far-reaching, affecting everything from individual species to entire ecosystems. It is imperative that we understand these effects and work to reduce our carbon emissions to prevent further damage to the ocean and the life it supports.
Section 3: The Impact on Indigenous Communities in Canada
Indigenous communities that depend on the ocean are especially vulnerable to the effects of ocean acidification. In this section, we will discuss the decline of important species like shellfish and the impact on their livelihood, culture, and food security.


For many Indigenous communities, shellfish are an important part of their culture and livelihood. But as ocean acidification continues, the decline in shellfish populations has severe consequences on their food security and cultural traditions.

(Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/nclimate2965 )


Additionally, many Indigenous communities rely on fishing for their income and subsistence. As key fish species become more scarce or migrate due to changes in the ocean chemistry, this can have a severe impact on their economic stability and food security.

(Source: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-319-46594-2_13 )


Furthermore, ocean acidification affects the entire marine ecosystem, which Indigenous communities often have deep connections to. This can have profound cultural impacts, disrupting traditional practices and ways of life, and leading to less resilience to those communities facing additional stressors.

(Source: https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1029/2011GB004155
In conclusion, Indigenous communities that depend on the ocean are disproportionately affected by ocean acidification. The decline of important species like shellfish not only affects their livelihoods and economic stability but also disrupts their cultural traditions and ways of life. As we work to address ocean acidification and climate change, it is essential to include the perspectives and voices of Indigenous communities to ensure that they are not left behind in our efforts towards sustainability and resilience.


(Source: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/285623008_The_Impact_of_Ocean_Acidification_on_Indigenous_Arctic_Communites )
Section 4: Possible Solutions to Ocean Acidification


The ocean is facing a critical threat from ocean acidification, which is caused by increased carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions. If we do not take immediate steps to reduce carbon emissions, the effects of ocean acidification will be more pronounced and widespread. In this section, we will discuss the various approaches that can be taken to mitigate the effects of ocean acidification in Canada.

One solution is to transition to renewable energy sources like wind, solar, or tidal power. By reducing carbon emissions, these sources of energy can help reduce the impact of ocean acidification. In Canada, there are several initiatives aimed at promoting the use of renewable energy, such as the federal government’s focus on investing in clean energy and supporting efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

(Source: https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/climate-change/canada-action-wind-energy.html )


Overfishing is another human activity that contributes to ocean acidification. By practicing sustainable fishing methods, we can minimize the negative impacts on marine ecosystems. In Canada, sustainable fishing practices are promoted by government policies and regulations, such as setting fishing quotas and closing fisheries when fish stocks are depleted. Additionally, there is increasing pressure on the fishing industry to adopt more sustainable practices to protect marine life and support local communities.

(Source: https://www.dfo-mpo.gc.ca/fisheries-peches/sustainable-durable/strategies-strategies-eng.html )


Conservation policies can be implemented to protect sensitive ecosystems and reduce stressors on marine species, such as reducing nutrient and chemical runoff from industries and agricultural activities. The Canadian government has implemented various conservation policies aimed at reducing the impact of human activities on marine life, such as the federal Oceans Act and other initiatives aimed at protecting marine ecosystems.

(Source: https://www.ec.gc.ca/cc-ocean/default.asp?lang=En&n=E65821F9-1 )



Finally, it is essential to invest in scientific research to better understand the impact of ocean acidification and to support innovations in technology and mitigation approaches. In Canada, there are several research initiatives investigating the impact of ocean acidification and strategies to reduce its impact. For example, the Department of Fisheries and Oceans has established an Ocean Acidification Program aimed at understanding the impacts of ocean acidification and developing adaptation strategies.

(Source: https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/climate-change/science-research-funding/ocean-acidification-research.html )

In conclusion, taking immediate steps to reduce carbon emissions, practicing sustainable fisheries, implementing conservation policies, and investing in scientific research can significantly reduce the impact of ocean acidification in Canada. These efforts are essential to protecting the ocean and the many species that depend on it.
Section 5: What Can You Do To Help?
Ocean acidification is a pressing issue that requires action from every individual. We can all make a difference by taking steps to reduce our carbon footprint. In this final section, we will provide tips that viewers can follow in their daily lives to reduce their carbon emissions and help prevent ocean acidification, as well as how they can support policies and organizations that are working to address climate change and protect the oceans.



One of the best ways to reduce your carbon footprint is to use alternative modes of transportation, such as biking, walking, or taking public transit whenever possible. This can significantly reduce the carbon emissions from vehicles, and it’s also a great way to stay active and healthy. Moreover, if you own a car, you can consider purchasing an electric vehicle, which produces zero carbon emissions during operation.

(Source: https://www.nrdc.org/stories/how-you-can-stop-global-warming )


Plastic pollution is another major issue that contributes to ocean acidification. To reduce your impact, consider reducing your use of single-use plastics like straws, water bottles, and plastic bags. Instead, try using reusable alternatives, such as a refillable water bottle or cloth bags for shopping. Additionally, you can use eco-friendly household and beauty products that use sustainable ingredients to minimize the plastic packaging waste.

(Source: https://www.wwf.ca/get-involved/plastic-pollution-canada )



Turning off lights and electronics when not in use is another simple way to reduce your carbon footprint. Not only will this reduce your energy consumption, but it will also lower your utility bills. Additionally, consider switching to energy-efficient LED light bulbs, which use up to 80% less energy and last up to 25 times longer than traditional bulbs.

(Source: https://www.epa.gov/energy/green-tips )



Finally, it’s essential to support policies and organizations working to address climate change and protect the oceans. This can involve advocating for policies aimed at reducing carbon emissions, such as carbon pricing or supporting initiatives aimed at protecting marine ecosystems. Additionally, you can volunteer or donate to organizations working towards climate action and lobby your elected representatives to take action on climate change.

(Source: https://www.nature.org/content/dam/tnc/nature/en/documents/Oceans-for-Climate-Report.pdf )

In conclusion, every individual can contribute to the efforts to reduce carbon emissions and prevent ocean acidification. Whether it’s reducing plastic use, using alternative modes of transportation, or supporting policies and organizations that promote a sustainable future, every action counts. By working together to protect our oceans and the life they support, we can help ensure a healthy planet for generations to come.


Conclusion:
Thank you for watching our video on ocean acidification. We hope you have learned more about this important topic and understand how we can all take action to make a positive impact on our oceans and the communities that depend on them. Remember, it’s not too late to make a difference!

import os
import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
from keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator
from keras.applications import ResNet50
from keras.layers import Dense, GlobalAveragePooling2D
from keras.models import Model
from keras.optimizers import Adam

# Parameters
num_classes = 2
image_size = 224

# Load the pre-trained ResNet-50 model
base_model = ResNet50(weights='imagenet', include_top=False, input_shape=(image_size, image_size, 3))

# Add custom layers on top of the pre-trained model
x = base_model.output
x = GlobalAveragePooling2D()(x)
x = Dense(1024, activation='relu')(x)
predictions = Dense(num_classes, activation='softmax')(x)

# Define the model with the pre-trained base and custom layers
model = Model(inputs=base_model.input, outputs=predictions)

# Freeze the base layers to avoid retraining them
for layer in base_model.layers:
    layer.trainable = False

# Compile the model
model.compile(optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=0.001), loss='categorical_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# Define the data generators for training and testing
train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1.0/255, shear_range=0.2, zoom_range=0.2, horizontal_flip=True)
test_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rescale=1.0/255)

train_generator = train_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    'dataset/training',
    target_size=(image_size, image_size),
    batch_size=32,
    class_mode='categorical')

test_generator = test_datagen.flow_from_directory(
    'dataset/validation',
    target_size=(image_size, image_size),
    batch_size=32,
    class_mode='categorical')

# Train the model
model.fit(
    train_generator,
    steps_per_epoch=train_generator.n // train_generator.batch_size,
    epochs=10,
    validation_data=test_generator,
    validation_steps=test_generator.n // test_generator.batch_size,
    workers=1)

# Save the model
model.save('models/astolfo_classifier_v1.h5')

Title: The Role of the Socialist System with Chinese Characteristics in China’s Rise as a Dominant Economic Power

Abstract

China’s meteoric rise as a critical player in the global economy is closely linked to its unique political system, the socialist system with Chinese characteristics. This research paper explores the intertwined relationship between China’s political framework and economic prowess, shedding light on how this system facilitated the nation’s emergence as a dominant economic power. We examine the key policies and initiatives enacted under this political structure, analyze its impact on China’s growth trajectory, and discuss the broader implications of China’s economic ascendance for the global community.

Introduction

China’s unprecedented economic growth over the past few decades has astonished the world, with the socialist system with Chinese characteristics playing a crucial role in shaping this development. The culmination of Marxist-Leninist principles and China’s historical and cultural context, this distinctive political ideology has driven extensive social, economic, and political reforms in the nation. This research paper investigates the role of the socialist system with Chinese characteristics in fostering China’s economic development, transforming the nation into a leading economic power, and the wider implications of this progress for the global community.

Methodology

To comprehensively analyze the impact of the socialist system with Chinese characteristics on China’s economic rise, we adopt a mixed-methods approach, involving historical analysis, literature review, case studies, and examination of primary sources like policy documents, economic data, and official speeches. These methods enable a thorough understanding of the relationship between China’s political and economic development.

Findings

1. Key Policies and Initiatives

The socialist system with Chinese characteristics has facilitated numerous transformative policies and initiatives, instrumental in accelerating China’s economic growth. Some of the crucial policies include the Reform and Opening-Up, the establishment of Special Economic Zones, and China’s accession to the World Trade Organization.

2. Economic Growth and Industrial Development

The political and economic environment fostered under the socialist system with Chinese characteristics facilitated rapid industrialization, urbanization, and an economic boom. The system’s emphasis on state-owned enterprises and strategic sectors, combined with the inclusion of market mechanisms, enabled remarkable growth rates and lifted millions of people out of poverty.

3. Global Integration and Influence

China’s economic ascendance under the unique socialist system has had significant implications for global trade patterns, supply chains, investment flows, and international institutions. China has increasingly become a source of capital, technology, and infrastructure development for countries across the globe, shaping the dynamics of the global economy.

Challenges and Opportunities

Despite the impressive economic progress, the socialist system with Chinese characteristics faces ongoing challenges:

1. Economic Imbalances and Inequality: Rapid economic growth has led to regional and income disparities within the country, a key challenge requiring attention to ensure sustainable and equitable development.

2. Trade Tensions and Dependency: As China’s economic power has grown, trade conflicts and perceptions of dependency on Chinese investment have become prevalent, requiring diplomacy and cooperation to address potential conflicts.

Conclusion

The socialist system with Chinese characteristics has been pivotal in laying the groundwork for China’s rise as a dominant economic power. A nuanced understanding of this unique political system, its influence on the Chinese economy, and the broader implications for the global landscape is critical to engage with China effectively. As China becomes increasingly central to the global economic narrative, addressing the challenges and leveraging opportunities inherent in its political-economic framework will be crucial for fostering productive international cooperation, trade, and innovation.

import requests

def basic_info():
    config = dict()
    config["access_token"] = st.secrets["access_token"]
    config['instagram_account_id'] = st.secrets.get("instagram_account_id", "")
    config["version"] = 'v16.0'
    config["graph_domain"] = 'https://graph.facebook.com/'
    config["endpoint_base"] = config["graph_domain"] + config["version"] + '/'
    return config

def InstaApiCall(url, params, request_type):
    if request_type == 'POST':
        req = requests.post(url, params)
    else:
        req = requests.get(url, params)
    res = dict()
    res["url"] = url
    res["endpoint_params"] = params
    res["endpoint_params_pretty"] = json.dumps(params, indent=4)
    res["json_data"] = json.loads(req.content)
    res["json_data_pretty"] = json.dumps(res["json_data"], indent=4)
    return res

def getUserMedia(params, pagingUrl=''):
    Params = dict()
    Params['fields'] = 'id,caption,media_type,media_url,permalink,thumbnail_url,timestamp,username,like_count,comments_count'
    Params['access_token'] = params['access_token']

    if not params['endpoint_base']:
        return None

    if pagingUrl == '':
        url = params['endpoint_base'] + params['instagram_account_id'] + '/media'
    else:
        url = pagingUrl

    return InstaApiCall(url, Params, 'GET')

def getUser(params):
    Params = dict()
    Params['fields'] = 'followers_count'
    Params['access_token'] = params['access_token']

    if not params['endpoint_base']:
        return None

    url = params['endpoint_base'] + params['instagram_account_id']

    return InstaApiCall(url, Params, 'GET')

def saveCount(count, filename):
    with open(filename, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(count, f, indent=4)

def getCount(filename):
    try:
        with open(filename, 'r') as f:
            return json.load(f)
    except (FileNotFoundError, json.decoder.JSONDecodeError):
        return {}

summarise this: 	Notes See Benaissa 2009. Although it was not common in traditional Greek religion to combine the names of two gods in this manner, the double determination of Hermanubis has some formal parallels in the earlier period. The most obvious is the god Ἑρμαφρόδιτοϲ (see Ajootian 1990), attested from the fourth century BC onwards, but his name implies the paradoxical union of two different gods (Hermes and Aphrodite) rather than an assimilation in the manner of Hermanubis. A more apt parallel is provided by Ζηνοποϲειδῶν, an epiclesis of the Carian god Zeus Osogoa, who received the additional determination "Poseidon" as a result of his representation with features such as the trident (see Blümel 1987, 128). In Egyptian religion, the tendency to unite the names of two gods was long established and is well known (cf. Leclant 1975). A comparable form to Hermanubis from the Roman period is Ἡλιοϲέραπιϲ, which is not attested as a personal name (pace Hopfner 1946, 45). Schwartz 1947 also postulates a syncretic funerary god Ἑρμηρακλῆϲ, but this is based purely on the iconographical evidence of some seals, without any support from textual sources (the personal name Ἑρμηρακλῆϲ, two instances of which have emerged since Schwartz's thesis, could be simply an example of a polytheophoric name and need not imply the recognition of an actual god of that name). The literary and epigraphic sources associating Anubis with Hermes are collected by Grenier 1977, 53–59, 95–98. Bernand, Inscriptions métriques n. 73 (Abydos; I–II) = GVI 1090, should also be added, as the Κυλλήνιοϲ Ἑρμῆϲ (9) leading the deceased to serve Osiris of Abydos is implicitly conflated with Anubis (see Dunand 1975, 158). Kákosy 1990, 145 points to some pre-Ptolemaic Egyptian statuettes, in which the feet or teeth of Thoth are modeled as jackal heads, as evidence that the later rapprochement of Anubis and Hermes was based on the earlier assimilation of Thoth to Anubis; but although Thoth and Anubis were closely associated in funerary contexts, I am not persuaded that these statuettes "wollten sicher den synkretischen Gott Thoth-Anubis darstellen" or that they are relevant to the question of the origin of the Graeco-Roman Hermanubis. See Leclant 1981, 871, and the references in Grenier 1977, 137–138. In the amulet published in Bonner 1950, n. 36 (Plate II) = LIMC I s.v. Anubis n. 19, Anubis is described as holding in his right hand "a tall scepter resembling a Roman military standard, with palm leaves at top, and two garlands, the lower with ties" (Bonner 1950, 259). But this looks to me like a version of the kerykeion-cum-palm known from a number of coins of the fully anthropomorphic Hermanubis; see Grenier 1990, 265, and cf. the coins under 8(a). Whether the appellation "Hermanubis" was given to the canine-headed Anubis with Hermetic features is uncertain; Apuleius, Met. XI 11, refers to the god attolens canis cernices arduas and laena caduceum gerens simply as "Anubis." LIMC I s.v. Anubis n. 13, found in Carthage or Cherchel, is the only representation for which an Alexandrian origin has been postulated, but on no firm basis (see Picard 1956, 176–179, who assigns to it an Alexandrian provenance and a date in the third century BC on the basis of the floral decoration). See Grenier 1990. Cf. Leclant 1981, 873. On "direct theonymy" see Parker 2000, 57–8, with further bibliography. For the accentuation Ἑρμάνουβιϲ rather than Ἑρμανοῦβιϲ (Preisigke and some editors), see Clarysse 1997, 180. While the letter was found in the Fayum, the sender is clearly writing from Alexandria; cf. Bagnall and Cribiore 2006, 389–390. For some observations on the marking of gender in Greek personal names, see Davies 2000, 20–21. Cf. Gignac, Grammar, II 55–57; Mayser, Grammatik, I.2 22–23. For masculine names in -ιϲ, -ιδοϲ/-ιοϲ and feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ, see Pape-Benseler, Wörterbuch der griechischen Eigennamen3, xviii-xix. See e.g. Preisigke, Namenbuch, 33–34; Foraboschi, Onomasticon, 34–35. See e.g. the "index des suffixes" in Masson 1990, II 632–633 s.v. -ᾶϲ ("suffixe d'hypocoristique et de sobriquet masculin").

(In the clubroom…)

Monika: “Wait, where’s Sayori?”

Natsuki: “I have no clue…”

Yuri: “I hope she’s okay…let’s wait until she actually shows up.”

Monika: “Agreed. We shouldn’t jump to conclusions just yet.”

Natsuki: “Yeah, maybe she’s just running a bit late. It’s not like her to miss club meetings without letting us know.”

Yuri: “But she seemed a little off yesterday…I hope everything is alright.”

(As the minutes tick by, the tension in the room grows. The club members couldn’t help but worry about Sayori’s absence.)

Monika: “I’ll try giving her a call.”

(Monika takes out her phone and dials Sayori’s number. After a few rings, it goes to voicemail.)

Monika: “Her phone is off…that’s not a good sign.”

(Just then, there’s a knock on the clubroom door.)

Yuri: “Maybe that’s her!”

(Natsuki goes to open the door, revealing Sayori standing there with a sheepish smile.)

Sayori: “Sorry, guys! I overslept this morning.”

Monika: “Oh, thank goodness you’re okay. We were really worried about you.”

Sayori: “I’m sorry for making you worry. I just had a really restless night and couldn’t wake up on time. BUUUUUUUT I have some good news to share with all of you! You know how MC and I got married last month?”

Monika: “Well…yeah. MC told us yesterday.”

Sayori: “Well, guess what? MC and I are expecting a baby! We just found out this morning!”

Natsuki: “Wait, seriously? That’s amazing!”

Yuri: “Congratulations, Sayori! That’s such wonderful news.”

Monika: “I’m so happy for you, Sayori. This is such a big milestone.”

Sayori: “Thank you, guys! We’re over the moon right now. We wanted to share the news with all of you as soon as possible.”

Natsuki: “And here we were, worrying about you oversleeping.”

Yuri: “I’m glad everything’s alright, Sayori. Congratulations to you both.”

Monika: “Indeed, this is such exciting news. We’ll all be here to support you throughout this journey.”

Sayori: “Thank you, everyone. Your support means the world to me. I knew I could count on you all.”

(They spend the rest of the club meeting discussing Sayori’s pregnancy, sharing their excitement and offering advice. The worry from earlier fades away as they focus on the joyous news. They celebrate this new chapter in Sayori and MC’s lives, cherishing the bond they’ve formed as friends.)

(Seven months later, and the club members are getting ready for the upcoming literature festival.)

Monika: “I can’t believe the festival is just around the corner. It feels like yesterday when Sayori shared her pregnancy news with us.”

Yuri: “Yes, time has flown by so quickly. I’m glad we’ve been able to support her throughout this journey.”

Natsuki: “I’m excited to see how their family grows. Sayori and MC are going to be amazing parents.”

Monika: “Absolutely. They have so much love to give, and I’m sure their child will be surrounded by happiness and warmth.”

(As they set up their literature club booth for the festival, Sayori arrives with a noticeable baby bump.)

Sayori: “Hi, everyone! Sorry, I’m a little late. Pregnancy slows me down a bit.”

Natsuki: “Look at you! You’re glowing, Sayori. How are you feeling?”

Sayori: “I’m doing great. The baby has been kicking a lot lately. It’s such a surreal feeling.”

Yuri: “I can only imagine. It’s an incredible miracle to bring a new life into the world.”

Monika: “We’re so proud of you, Sayori. You’ve embraced this journey with so much joy and positivity.”

Sayori: “Thanks, guys. I couldn’t have done it without your support. And MC has been amazing too.”

(Throughout the festival, the club members take turns watching over the booth while Sayori rests and takes breaks. They receive many visitors and share their love for literature with the attendees.)

Yuri: “It’s great to see so many people interested in our club. We’ve really made an impact.”

Natsuki: “Yeah, despite all the ups and downs, we’ve come a long way. And Sayori’s baby is going to inherit this amazing legacy.”

Monika: “The literature club will continue to grow and evolve, just like our friendship. We’ll always support each other, no matter what.”

Sayori: (looking down at her baby bump, then clutching it) “Oh! Oh…oh…”

Monika: “Sayori, what’s wrong? Are you okay?”

Sayori: “I…I think I’m having contractions.”

Yuri: “Contractions? Oh my goodness, is the baby coming?”

Natsuki: “H-H-Hang on! I-I-I’ll call MC!”

(Meanwhile, Natsuki quickly dials MC’s number, her hands trembling with nervousness.)

Natsuki: “Come on, come on, please pick up!”

MC: “Hello? Natsuki, is that you?”

Natsuki: “MC, it’s urgent! Sayori is having contractions, and I think the baby is coming!”

MC: “What!? I’ll be right there!”

(Sayori’s face is filled with a mix of excitement and anxiety as the club members try to calm her down and make her comfortable.)

Monika: “Just breathe, Sayori. Everything will be alright. MC is on his way.”

Yuri: “We’re here for you, Sayori. We’ll support you through this.”

Natsuki: “Stay strong, Sayori. MC will be here soon.”

(Soon enough, MC bursts through the door of the literature club, holding a bag with essentials for the hospital.)

MC: “Sayori, are you okay?”

Sayori: (gritting her teeth) “MC…the contractions are getting stronger…”

(Just then, Sayori’s water breaks, signaling that she’s fully dilated and ready to push.)

Sayori: “Oh gosh, it’s happening! The baby is coming!”

MC: (taking Sayori’s hand) “You can do this, Sayori. I’m here with you every step of the way.”

Monika: “Let’s help prepare Sayori for delivery. We need to keep her as comfortable as possible.”

(Yuri and Natsuki quickly gather blankets and pillows, creating a makeshift bed for Sayori to lie on.)

Sayori: “I can’t believe this is actually happening…”

MC: “You’re doing amazing, Sayori. Just keep breathing and pushing.”

(Natsuki and Yuri hold Sayori’s hands, providing encouragement and support as she continues to go through labor.)

Monika: “We’re all here for you, Sayori. Just remember that you’re strong and capable.”

(Sayori pushes with all her might, her determination fueling her through the pain and exhaustion.)

MC: “I can see the baby’s head. Just a few more pushes, Sayori.”

Yuri: “You’re almost there, Sayori. Just a little bit longer.”

(Sayori lets out one final push, and with a cry, the sound of a newborn baby fills the clubroom.)

Monika: “It’s a girl!”

Sayori: (teary-eyed) “MC…we have a daughter…our beautiful baby girl.”

MC: (overwhelmed with emotion) “She’s perfect, Sayori. We did it.”

Natsuki: “Awww, she’s so tiny and cute! Congratulations, you two. What are you going to name her?”

Sayori: “Sky. Our sweet little Sky.”

Yuri: “What a beautiful name, Sayori. Sky suits her perfectly.”

Monika: “She’s going to grow up surrounded by so much love and support.”

Natsuki: “I’m already looking forward to seeing her at future literature club meetings.”

Sayori: “Thank you, everyone. Your presence and support have meant the world to me throughout this journey. I couldn’t ask for better friends.”

MC: “We’re so grateful to have all of you in our lives. Thank you for being here for us.”

(As the club members come together to celebrate the birth of Sky, they cherish the bond they’ve formed and the future ahead. The literature club will continue to thrive, just like the growing family it has become.)

def step9_create_product_table(data_filename, normalized_database_filename):
    # Inputs: Name of the data and normalized database filename
    # Output: None

    
    ### BEGIN SOLUTION
    with open(data_filename, 'r') as f:
        header = f.readline().split('\t')
        prodCat_col_index = header.index("ProductCategory")
        prodName_col_index = header.index("ProductName")
        prodUP_col_index = header.index("ProductUnitPrice")
        prodCatID_dict = step8_create_productcategory_to_productcategoryid_dictionary(normalized_database_filename)
        product_table = set()
        
        for line in f.readlines():
            prodCatID = [ prodCatID_dict[i] for i in (line.split('\t')[prodCat_col_index]).split(';') ]
            product_table.update( zip( (line.split('\t')[prodName_col_index]).split(';') , map(float,(line.split('\t')[prodUP_col_index]).split(';') ), prodCatID  ) )
        product_table = [ [i+1,*j] for i,j in enumerate(sorted(product_table))]  
        
    conn = create_connection(normalized_database_filename)
    
    with conn:
        create_region_table = '''CREATE TABLE Product( [ProductID] integer not null Primary key,
                                                        [ProductName] Text not null,
                                                        [ProductUnitPrice] Real not null,
                                                        [ProductCategoryID] integer not null,
                                                        foreign key (ProductCategoryID) References ProductCategory (ProductCategoryID) );'''
                                    
        create_table(conn,create_region_table,'Product')
        
        conn.cursor().executemany("INSERT INTO Product VALUES (?,?,?,?) ", product_table)
   
    ### END SOLUTION

make me a summary of this material  In hydraulic cylinders the most commonly
used guides are guide rings and guide strips.
They accommodate radial loads of forces acting on the cylinder assembly and guide the rod
in the cylinder head as well as the piston in the
cylinder bore († fig. 1).
Guides are made of polymer materials and
prevent metal-to-metal contact between
moving parts in a working hydraulic cylinder.
Compared to metal guides, polymer guides
provide the following advantages in hydraulic
cylinders:
•	 significantly longer service life
•	 work more smoothly against the cylinder
bore and the sealing surfaces
•	 avoid wear of cylinder surfaces despite the
presence of contamination particles
•	 high resistance to insufficient lubrication at
low speeds
•	 larger contact area († fig. 2, page 251 and
fig. 4, page 254) due to higher degree of
elastic deformation distributes the load and
reduces stress to counter-surface
•	 certain self-lubricating properties
SKF supplies different precision machined
guide rings with different polymer materials
and sizes, which make them appropriate for
a wide variety of operating conditions and
applications.
Guides of PTFE are also available for applications where start-up friction must be minimized. PTFE guides have limited load carrying
capability and should only be used in applications with light loads.
Guide lubrication
The guide must receive ample lubrication at all
times. Rod guides are typically placed inward
of both the rod and buffer seal and should be
lubricated on assembly with the same medium
as used in the system. SKF recommends to
place guides not outside of the rod seal, means
between the wiper and rod seal. However, in
certain conditions, PTFE guides may be used
outside the rod seal due to their certain selflubricating properties.

I have a access database named portfolio, storing the portfolio and benchmark information. the portfolio table contains 6 columns, “portfolio ID”, “tradedate”, “ticker”, “weight”, “price change” and “industry”. the benchmark table also has 6 columns: “bench ID”, “tradedate”, “ticker”, “weight”, “price change”, “industry”. please use python define a function brinson (portfolioID , bench ID, begindate, enddate), setup connection to the database, calculate and return the total daily excess return and each effect, and plot a chart in the end as well.   Be careful, First, I need to run the brinson attribution grouping by industry. I recommand you using 4 additional serials, you can call it Q1 Q2 Q3 and Q4. Q1= sigma benchmark weight * benchmark return in each industry. Q2= sigma portfolio weight * benchmark return in each industry. Q3= sigma benchmark weight * portfolio return in each industry. Q4= sigma portfolio weight * portfolio return in each industry. As you can see, excess return=Q4 - Q1, allocation effect = Q2-Q1, Selection effect =Q3-Q1, and interaction effect= Q4 - Q3 - Q2 + Q1. Second, When running multi-period attribution. the excess rtn should be compounded correctly. For example when calculation the total effect of day1 to day2. you have the Q1 for day1 and Q1 for day2, then total Q1=Q1 day1 + (1+Q1 day1)*Q1 day2. then used the conpound Q values, you will get the right compund excess return and attribution result.

Mindy: (worriedly) “Please tell me they’ll work! I can’t afford to lose my best friend to my younger sister!”

Mindy: “Alright…here goes…”

(In the lair of Vanessa X)

Vanessa X: (turns around) “Well, well, well…if it isn’t my older sister. I’ve been wondering when you’d show up.”

Mindy: “Vanessa…this isn’t like you!”

Vanessa X: “Oh…but it is. I’m fine being Vanessa X. After all, my offspring keep coming week after week. And speaking of which, it looks like my next batch is ready to be born…”

Mindy: “No! I won’t let you give birth to any more monsters!” (throws the beige potion at Vanessa X)

Vanessa X: (slashes the potion in two) “Nice try, Mindy. But you won’t stop me that easily.”

Mizore: (steps forward) “Try this one instead.” (throws the red potion at Vanessa X)

Vanessa X: (also slashes it in two) “You really think these pathetic potions can stop me? You underestimate my power. Now if you’ll excuse me, I must get ready for the arrival of my next batch.”

Vanessa: (tearfully) “Thank you…thank you so much.”

Mindy: “And we’ll be there for you every step of the way.”
