How many tokens are there below?: ## Chapter 1 - Laying the Foundation > The basics of OrbitDB include installing OrbitDB (and IPFS), setting up an isomorphic project that runs in both Node.js and the browser, creating databases, and understanding how to choose datastores.

Table of Contents

Please see the [Introduction](./00_Introduction.md) before beginning this chapter. - [Installing the requirements: IPFS and OrbitDB](#installing-the-requirements-ipfs-and-orbitdb) - [Instantiating IPFS and OrbitDB](#instantiating-ipfs-and-orbitdb) - [Creating a database](#creating-a-database) - [Choosing a datastore](#choosing-a-data-store) - [Key takeaways](#key-takeaways)
### Installing the requirements: IPFS and OrbitDB Before you start working on the core functionality behind the peer-to-peer sharing of sheet music, you’ll need to get the foundations of our distributed database set up. You will need to get the code for OrbitDB and its dependency, IPFS, and make it available to your project. The process is different between the browser and Node.js, so we cover both here. > **Note**: Both OrbitDB and js-ipfs are open source, which give you the ability to build and even contribute to the code. This will be covered in detail in [Part 3](../03_The_Architecture_of_OrbitDB). #### In Node.js Choose a project directory and `cd` to there from your command line. Then run the following command: ```bash $ npm init --yes # use npm defaults, you can edit this later $ npm install --save orbit-db ipfs ``` This will create a `package.json`, `package-lock.json`, and `node_modules` folder. > **Note:** If you're running on a Windows prompt, or if you don't have certain build tools like [`g++`](https://gcc.gnu.org) and [`python`](https://www.python.org) installed, you may see a noisy console output with lots of warnings and errors. Keep going, your code should still run. If you want to use Git to track your progress, we also suggest the following: ```bash $ git init $ echo node_modules > .gitignore ``` Of course, be careful before copying and pasting any commands anyone ever tells you into your terminal. If you don't understand a command, figure out what it is supposed to do, before copying it over. Copy and paste at your own risk. > **Note:** This code was tested on Node v11.14.0. Your mileage for other versions may vary. #### In the Browser If you're using the browser for this tutorial, we recommend using [unpkg](https://www.unpkg.com) for obtaining pre-built, minified versions of both IPFS and OrbitDB. Simply include these in your HTML: ```html ``` You will now have global `Ipfs` and `OrbitDB` objects available to you. You will see how we'll use these later. ### Creating the isomorphic bookends Since OrbitDB works in the browser and Node.js, you're going to want to make the library as _isomorphic_ as possible. This means we want the same code to run in the browser as runs in REPL or local environment. Luckily, you will have the luxury of using the same language, JavaScript, for both Node.js and browser environments. Create a new file called `newpieceplease.js` and put this code in there: ```js class NewPiecePlease { constructor (Ipfs, OrbitDB) { this.Ipfs = Ipfs this.OrbitDB = OrbitDB } } try { const Ipfs = require('ipfs') const OrbitDB = require('orbit-db') module.exports = exports = new NewPiecePlease(Ipfs, OrbitDB) } catch (e) { window.NPP = new NewPiecePlease(window.Ipfs, window.OrbitDB) } ``` Source: [GitHub](https://github.com/orbitdb/field-manual/blob/a5459ac56402f620cab424c6c399f7c593e94f85/code_examples/01_01_newpieceplease.js), or on IPFS at `QmRZycUKy3MnRKRxkLu8jTzBEVHZovsYcbhdiwLQ221eBP`. In the browser, you can include this file in a script tag and have an `NPP` object at your disposal. In Node.js, you can simply call something like: ```plain $ node > const NPP = require('./newpieceplease') ``` Not much should happen either way, since there's not much code there yet. For now just make sure you can create the `NPP` constant. #### What just happened? Using some key JavaScript features, you have created the shell for our application that runs in both Node.js and the browser. It defines a new class called `NewPiecePlease`, with a constructor that takes two arguments 1. `IPFS` for the `js-ipfs` constructor 2. `OrbitDB` for the `orbit-db` constructor From here on out, we will ignore these isometric bookends and concentrate wholly on the `NewPiecePlease` class. ### Instantiating IPFS and OrbitDB OrbitDB requires a running IPFS node to operate, so you will create one here and notify OrbitDB about it. > **Note:** We have designed Chapters 1 and 2 of the tutorial to work offline, not requiring any internet connectivity or connections to peers. ```js class NewPiecePlease { constructor (Ipfs, OrbitDB) { this.OrbitDB = OrbitDB this.Ipfs = Ipfs } async create() { this.node = await this.Ipfs.create({ preload: { enabled: false }, repo: './ipfs', EXPERIMENTAL: { pubsub: true }, config: { Bootstrap: [], Addresses: { Swarm: [] } } }) this._init() } async _init () { this.orbitdb = await this.OrbitDB.createInstance(this.node) this.defaultOptions = { accessController: { write: [this.orbitdb.identity.id] } } if(this.onready) this.onready(); } } ``` Source: [GitHub](../code_examples/01_Tutorial/02_Managing_Data). This allows you to run something like the following in your application code: ```JavaScript NPP.onready = () => { console.log(NPP.orbitdb.identity.id) } NPP.create() ```

summarize this "Fat also begins to infiltrate your abdomen, accumulating in between your
organs. Where subcutaneous fat is thought to be relatively harmless, this
“visceral fat” is anything but. These fat cells secrete inflammatory cytokines
such as TNF-alpha and IL-6, key markers and drivers of inflammation, in
close proximity to your most important bodily organs. This may be why
visceral fat is linked to increased risk of both cancer and cardiovascular
disease.
Fat storage capacity varies widely among individuals. Going back to our
tub analogy, some people have subcutaneous fat-storage capacity equivalent
to a regular bathtub, while others may be closer to a full-sized Jacuzzi or hot
tub. Still others may have only the equivalent of a five-gallon bucket. It also
matters, obviously, how much “water”is flowing into the tub via the faucet (as
calories in food) and how much is flowing out via the drain (or being
consumed via exercise or other means).
Individual fat-storage capacity seems to be influenced by genetic factors.
This is a generalization, but people of Asian descent (for example), tend to
have much lower capacity to store fat, on average, than Caucasians. There are
other factors at play here as well, but this explains in part why some people
can be obese but metabolically healthy, while others can appear “skinny”
while still walking around with three or more markers of metabolic
syndrome. It’s these people who are most at risk, according to research by
Mitch Lazar at the University of Pennsylvania, because a “thin” person may
simply have a much lower capacity to safely store fat. All other things being
equal, someone who carries a bit of body fat may also have greater fat-storage
capacity, and thus more metabolic leeway than someone who appears to be
more lean.
It doesn’t take much visceral fat to cause problems. Let’s say you are a
forty-year-old man who weighs two hundred pounds. If you have 20 percent
body fat, making you more or less average (50th percentile) for your age and
sex, that means you are carrying 40 pounds of fat throughout your body. Even
if just 4.5 pounds of that is visceral fat, you would be considered at
exceptionally high risk for cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes, in the
top 5 percent of risk for your age and sex. This is why I insist my patients
undergo a DEXA scan annually—and I am far more interested in their
visceral fat than their total body fat.
—
It may have taken you a long time to get there, but now you are in trouble—
even if you, and your doctor, may not yet realize it. You have fat
accumulating in many places where it should not be, such as in your liver,
between your abdominal organs, even around your heart—regardless of your
actual weight. But one of the first places where this overflowing fat will cause
problems is in your muscle, as it worms its way in between your muscle
fibers, like marbling on a steak. As this continues, microscopic fat droplets
even appear inside your muscle cells.
This is where insulin resistance likely begins, Gerald Shulman concludes
from three decades’ worth of investigation. These fat droplets may be among
the first destinations of excess energy/fat spillover, and as they accumulate
they begin to disrupt the complex network of insulin-dependent transport
mechanisms that normally bring glucose in to fuel the muscle cell. When
these mechanisms lose their function, the cell becomes “deaf” to insulin’s
signals. Eventually, this insulin resistance will progress to other tissues, such
as the liver, but Shulman believes that it originates in muscle. It’s worth
noting that one key ingredient in this process seems to be inactivity. If a
person is not physically active, and they are not consuming energy via their
muscles, then this fat-spillover-driven insulin resistance develops much more
quickly. (This is why Shulman requires his study subjects, mostly young
college students, to refrain from physical activity, in order to push them
towards insulin resistance.)
Insulin resistance is a term that we hear a lot, but what does it really
mean? Technically, it means that cells, initially muscle cells, have stopped
listening to insulin’s signals, but another way to visualize it is to imagine the
cell as a balloon being blown up with air. Eventually, the balloon expands to
the point where it gets more difficult to force more air inside. You have to
blow harder and harder. This is where insulin comes in, to help facilitate the
process of blowing air into the balloon. The pancreas begins to secrete even
more insulin, to try to remove excess glucose from the bloodstream and cram
it into cells. For the time being it works, and blood glucose levels remain
normal, but eventually you reach a limit where the “balloon” (cells) cannot
accept any more “air” (glucose).
This is when the trouble shows up on a standard blood test, as fasting
blood glucose begins to rise. This means you have high insulin levels and high
blood glucose, and your cells are shutting the gates to glucose entry. If things
continue in this way, then the pancreas becomes fatigued and less able to
mount an insulin response. This is made worse by, you guessed it, the fat now
residing in the pancreas itself. You can see the vicious spiral forming here: fat
spillover helps initiate insulin resistance, which results in the accumulation of
still more fat, eventually impairing our ability to store calories as anything
other than fat. There are many other hormones involved in the production and
distribution of fat, including testosterone, estrogen, hormone-sensitive
lipase[*3] and cortisol. Cortisol is especially potent, with a double-edged effect
of depleting subcutaneous fat (which is generally beneficial) and replacing it
with more harmful visceral fat. This is one reason why stress levels and sleep,
both of which affect cortisol release, are pertinent to metabolism. But insulin
seems to be the most potent as far as promoting fat accumulation because it
acts as kind of a one-way gate, allowing fat to enter the cell while impairing
the release of energy from fat cells (via a process called lipolysis). Insulin is
all about fat storage, not fat utilization.
When insulin is chronically elevated, more problems arise. Fat gain and
ultimately obesity are merely one symptom of this condition, known as
hyperinsulinemia. I would argue that they are hardly even the most serious
symptoms: as we’ll see in the coming chapters, insulin is also a potent growthsignaling hormone that helps foster both atherosclerosis and cancer. And
when insulin resistance begins to develop, the train is already well down the
track toward type 2 diabetes, which brings a multitude of unpleasant
consequences.
—
Our slowly dawning awareness of NAFLD and NASH mirrors the emergence
of the global epidemic of type 2 diabetes a century ago. Like cancer,
Alzheimer’s, and heart disease, type 2 diabetes is known as a “disease of
civilization,” meaning it has only come to prominence in the modern era.
Among primitive tribes and in prior times, it was largely unknown. Its
symptoms had been recognized for thousands of years, going back to ancient
Egypt (as well as ancient India), but it was the Greek physician Aretaeus of
Cappadocia who named it diabetes, describing it as “a melting down of the
flesh and limbs into urine.”
Back then, it was vanishingly rare, observed only occasionally. As type 2
diabetes emerged, beginning in the early 1700s, it was at first largely a disease
of the superelite, popes and artists and wealthy merchants and nobles who
could afford this newly fashionable luxury food known as sugar. The
composer Johann Sebastian Bach is thought to have been afflicted, among
other notable personages. It also overlapped with gout, a more commonly
recognized complaint of the decadent upper classes. This, as we’ll soon see,
was not a coincidence.
By the early twentieth century, diabetes was becoming a disease of the
masses. In 1940 the famed diabetologist Elliott Joslin estimated that about
one person in every three to four hundred was diabetic, representing an
enormous increase from just a few decades earlier, but it was still relatively
uncommon. By 1970, around the time I was born, its prevalence was up to
one in every fifty people. Today over 11 percent of the US adult population,
one in nine, has clinical type 2 diabetes, according to a 2022 CDC report,
including more than 29 percent of adults over age sixty-five. Another 38
percent of US adults—more than one in three—meet at least one of the
criteria for prediabetes. That means that nearly half of the population is either
on the road to type 2 diabetes or already there.
One quick note: diabetes ranks as only the seventh or eighth leading cause
of death in the United States, behind things like kidney disease, accidents,
and Alzheimer’s disease. In 2020, a little more than one hundred thousand
deaths were attributed to type 2 diabetes, a fraction of the number due to
either cardiovascular disease or cancer. By the numbers, it barely qualifies as
a Horseman. But I believe that the actual death toll due to type 2 diabetes is
much greater and that we undercount its true impact. Patients with diabetes
have a much greater risk of cardiovascular disease, as well as cancer and
Alzheimer’s disease and other dementias; one could argue that diabetes with
related metabolic dysfunction is one thing that all these conditions have in
common. This is why I place such emphasis on metabolic health, and why I
have long been concerned about the epidemic of metabolic disease not only
in the United States but around the world.
Why is this epidemic happening now?
The simplest explanation is likely that our metabolism, as it has evolved
over millennia, is not equipped to cope with our ultramodern diet, which has
appeared only within the last century or so. Evolution is no longer our friend,
because our environment has changed much faster than our genome ever
could. Evolution wants us to get fat when nutrients are abundant: the more
energy we could store, in our ancestral past, the greater our chances of
survival and successful reproduction. We needed to be able to endure periods
of time without much food, and natural selection obliged, endowing us with
genes that helped us conserve and store energy in the form of fat. That
enabled our distant ancestors to survive periods of famine, cold climates, and
physiologic stressors such as illness and pregnancy. But these genes have
proved less advantageous in our present environment, where many people in
the developed world have access to almost unlimited calories.
Another problem is that not all of these calories are created equal, and not
all of them are metabolized in the same way. One abundant source of calories
in our present diet, fructose, also turns out to be a very powerful driver of
metabolic dysfunction if consumed to excess. Fructose is not a novel nutrient,
obviously. It’s the form of sugar found in nearly all fruits, and as such it is
essential in the diets of many species, from bats and hummingbirds up to
bears and monkeys and humans. But as it turns out, we humans have a unique
capacity for turning calories from fructose into fat.
Lots of people like to demonize fructose, especially in the form of highfructose corn syrup, without really understanding why it’s supposed to be so
harmful. The story is complicated but fascinating. The key factor here is that
fructose is metabolized in a manner different from other sugars. When we
metabolize fructose, along with certain other types of foods, it produces large
amounts of uric acid, which is best known as a cause of gout but which has
also been associated with elevated blood pressure."

summarize: These are more rigorous than epidemiology, and they offer some ability to
infer causality thanks to the process of randomization, but they too are often
flawed. There’s a trade-off between sample size, study duration, and control.
To do a long study in a large group of subjects, you essentially have to trust
that they are following the prescribed diet, whether the Big Mac diet in our
hypothetical example from above, or a simple low-fat diet. If you want to
ensure that your subjects are actually eating the diet, you need to feed each
subject, observe them eating, and keep them locked in the metabolic ward of
a hospital (to be sure they are not eating anything else). All of this is doable,
but only for a handful of subjects for a few weeks at a time, which is not
nearly a large enough sample or long enough duration to infer anything
beyond mechanistic insights about nutrients and health.
These studies make pharmaceutical studies seem simple. Determining
whether pill X lowers blood pressure enough to prevent heart attacks requires
only that study subjects remember to take their pill every day for however
many months or years, and even that simple compliance poses a challenge.
Now imagine trying to ensure that study subjects lower their dietary fat
content to no more than 20 percent of total calories and consume at least five
servings of fruits and vegetables daily for a year. In fact, I’m convinced that
compliance is the key issue in nutrition research, and with diets in general:
Can you stick to it? The answer is different for almost everyone. This is why
it’s so difficult for experiments to answer the central questions about the
relationship between diet and disease, no matter how big and ambitious they
are.
One classic example of a well-intended nutrition study that created more
confusion than clarity is the Women’s Health Initiative (WHI), an enormous
randomized controlled trial that was meant to test a low-fat, high-fiber diet in
nearly fifty thousand women. Begun in 1993, it lasted eight years and cost
nearly $750 million (and if it sounds familiar, that is because of the study’s
highly publicized other arm, discussed earlier, which looked at the effects of
hormone replacement therapy on older women). In the end, despite all this
effort, the WHI found no statistically significant difference between the lowfat and control diet groups in terms of incidence of breast cancer, colorectal
cancer, cardiovascular disease, or overall mortality.
[*6]
Many people, myself included, argued that the results of this study
demonstrated the lack of efficacy of low-fat diets. But in reality, it probably
told us nothing about a low-fat diet because the “low-fat” intervention group
consumed around 28 percent of their calories from fat, while the control
group got about 37 percent of their calories from fat. (And that’s even
assuming the investigators were able to be remotely accurate in their
assessment of what the subjects actually ate over the years, a big assumption.)
So this study compared two diets that were pretty similar, and found that they
had pretty similar outcomes. Big surprise. Nevertheless, flawed as it was, the
WHI study has been fought over for years by partisans of different ways of
eating.
Just as an aside, the WHI study does provide a clear example of why it is
so important to evaluate any intervention, nutritional or otherwise, through
the lens of efficacy versus effectiveness. Efficacy tests how well the
intervention works under perfect conditions and adherence (i.e., if one does
everything exactly as prescribed). Effectiveness tests how well the
intervention works under real-world conditions, in real people. Most people
confuse these and therefore fail to appreciate this nuance of clinical trials.
The WHI was not a test of the efficacy of a low-fat diet for the simple reasons
that (a) it failed to test an actual low-fat diet, and (b) study subjects did not
adhere to the diet perfectly. So it can’t be argued from the WHI that low-fat
diets do not improve health, only that the prescription of a low-fat diet, in this
population of patients, did not improve health. See the difference?
—
That said, some clinical trials have provided some useful bits of knowledge.
One of the best, or least bad, clinical trials ever executed seemed to show a
clear advantage for the Mediterranean diet—or at least, for nuts and olive oil.
This study also focused on the role of dietary fats.
The large Spanish study known as PREDIMED (PREvención con DIeta
MEDiterránea) was elegant in its design: rather than telling the nearly 7,500
subjects exactly what they were supposed to eat, the researchers simply gave
one group a weekly “gift” of a liter of olive oil, which was meant to nudge
them toward other desired dietary changes (i.e., to eat the sorts of things that
one typically prepares with olive oil). A second group was given a quantity of
nuts each week and told to eat an ounce per day, while the control group was
simply instructed to eat a lower-fat diet, with no nuts, no excess fat on the
meat they did eat, no sofrito (a garlicky Spanish tomato sauce with onions and
peppers that sounds delicious), and weirdly, no fish.
The study was meant to last six years, but in 2013 the investigators
announced that they had halted it prematurely, after just four and a half years,
because the results were so dramatic. The group receiving the olive oil had
about a one-third lower incidence (31 percent) of stroke, heart attack, and
death than the low-fat group, and the mixed-nuts group showed a similar
reduced risk (28 percent). It was therefore deemed unethical to continue the
low-fat arm of the trial. By the numbers, the nuts-or-olive-oil
“Mediterranean” diet appeared to be as powerful as statins, in terms of
number needed to treat (NNT), for primary prevention of heart disease—
meaning in a population that had not yet experienced an “event” or a clinical
diagnosis.
[*7]
It looked like a slam dunk; it’s rare when investigators can report hard
outcomes like death or heart attack, as opposed to simple weight loss, in a
mere dietary study. It did help that the subjects already had at least three
serious risk factors, such as type 2 diabetes, smoking, hypertension, elevated
LDL-C, low HDL-C, overweight or obesity, or a family history of premature
coronary heart disease. Yet despite their elevated risk, the olive oil (or nuts)
diet had clearly helped them delay disease and death. A post hoc analysis of
PREDIMED data also found cognitive improvement in those allocated the
Mediterranean-style diet(s), versus cognitive decline in those allocated the
low-fat diet.
But does that mean a Mediterranean diet is right for everyone, or that
extra-virgin olive oil is the healthiest type of fat? Possibly—but not
necessarily.
To me, perhaps the most vexing issue with diet and nutrition studies is the
degree of variation between individuals that is found but often obscured. This
is especially true in studies looking mostly or entirely at weight loss as an end
point. The published studies report average results that are almost always
underwhelming, subjects losing a few pounds on average. In reality, some
individuals may have lost quite a bit of weight on the diet, while others lost
none or even gained weight.
There are two issues at play here. The first is compliance: how well can
you stick to the diet? That differs for everyone; we all have different behaviors
and thought patterns around food. The second issue is how a given diet affects
you, with your individual metabolism and other risk factors. Yet these are too
often ignored, and we end up with generalizations about how diets “don’t
work.” What that really means is that diet X or diet Y doesn’t work for
everyone.
Our goal in the next chapter is to help you figure out the best eating plan
for you, as an individual. To do that, we must move beyond labels and dive
into nutritional biochemistry.

“That’s uncalled for, Anna… You wouldn’t begrudge me a hug, would you?” Anna glared at Jon with mixed emotions, her eyes shooting daggers.

“If you must… suppose that’s right!”

“Bro, don’t start any nonsense… You feel me?” Anna said, her voice cracking with a sharp, piercing intensity.

“You’re right… Education is important… I do not want to conform to the conservative and traditional mindset of individuals within this household… I vehemently refuse to adhere to their ways… If you have a dislike towards it, I suggest you proceed to a location deemed unpleasant.”

Adding insult to injury, Anna shouted, “Let me be true to myself…” her words reverberating with an echoing intensity that filled the room.

“What is happening in this place?.. This is beyond my comprehension!” Jake said. The tension was palpable, and Jake noticed Anna seething with anger, her breaths coming out in short, sharp bursts. Trying to diffuse the situation, he inquired, “Anna, can you please clarify your current actions?”

Meanwhile, Jon received a call from Flight Sergeant James. “Hey,” Jon answered.

“I’m sorry to bother you at home, Sir… but I can’t locate your report from the 12th,” Flight Sergeant James said, his voice crackling over the phone line.

(In the clubroom…)

Monika: “Hmm…let’s se here…I’ve got baby clothes, clean diapers, binkies…” (rubbing her baby bump) “Everything prepared for when my little Minika arrives in two months!”

(Just then, the other club members enter the clubroom - confused as to what Monika is talking about.)

Sayori: “Uh…Monika, are you okay? What are you talking about?”

Monika: (looking slightly embarrassed) “Oh, sorry. I forgot to mention it to you all. I’m pregnant. I’m going to be a mom soon!”

Natsuki: (blinking in disbelief) “Wait, how did this happen? I mean, didn’t we all just start our second year of high school?”

Monika: “Well, sometimes life surprises you, Natsuki. It was unexpected, but I’m excited about it.”

Yuri: (looking concerned) “Monika, have you told your parents yet? How are they reacting?”

Monika: “I told them a few days ago. At first, they were a bit shocked, but they’re supportive. They said they’ll help me through this.”

Sayori: (with a smile) “That’s great, Monika! I’m sure you’re going to be an amazing mom.”

Monika: (grateful) “Thank you, Sayori. That means a lot to me. I want to do my best for my baby.”

Natsuki: (still processing the news) “I can’t believe Monika is going to be a mom. It feels surreal.”

Yuri: (softly) “It’s a big responsibility, but I have faith that Monika will handle it with grace and care.”

Monika: (nodding) “Thank you, Yuri. I will do my best to prepare myself and provide a loving and nurturing environment for my child.”

Sayori: (thoughtfully) “We should all help Monika whenever she needs it. We’re a club, and we support each other, right?”

Natsuki: (smiling) “Yeah, we’ll be here for you, Monika. Just don’t expect me to change any diapers, though.”

Monika: (laughs) “I won’t hold you to that, Natsuki. But having your support means a lot to me.”

Yuri: “Is there anything we can do to assist you during your pregnancy, Monika? Researching baby care or offering advice?”

Monika: “That would be wonderful, Yuri. I’m new to all of this, so any information or guidance is greatly appreciated.” (clutching her baby bump) “Aah…ow…! N-N-Not yet, Minika…it’s too early!”

Sayori: (concerned) “Monika, are you okay?!”

Monika: (breathing heavily) “I-I’m fine, just a little scare…I think the baby just kicked. It’s getting active in there.”

Natsuki: (rushing to Monika’s side) “You sure you’re alright? Should we call a doctor or something?”

Monika: (regaining her composure) “No, no need for that. It’s just a normal part of pregnancy. Sometimes the baby’s movements can be a bit surprising. I’m fine, really.”

Yuri: (placing a hand on Monika’s shoulder) “If you ever feel worried or experience any discomfort, please let us know immediately. We don’t want to take any chances.”

Monika: (reassured) “Thank you, Yuri. I appreciate your concern and support. Having you all here means a lot to me.”

Sayori: (gently rubbing Monika’s back) “You know, Monika, I’ve read that talking to your baby and playing gentle music can be soothing for both of you. Maybe we can find some lullabies or nursery rhymes for you to listen to.”

Monika: (smiling) “That’s a wonderful idea, Sayori. I’ll definitely give it a try. I want to do everything I can to foster a loving bond with my baby.” (feeling another intense wave of pain) “Oww!! Okay…probably not the baby kicking…”

Sayori: (worried) “Monika, are you sure you’re okay?! We should call for help!”

Monika: (gritting her teeth) “No, it’s…it’s probably just Braxton Hicks contractions. They’re common during pregnancy. I just need to take some deep breaths and wait for it to pass.”

Natsuki: (frantically) “Are you sure?! We can’t just stand here and do nothing!”

Yuri: (calmly) “Let’s try to keep Monika comfortable and help her relax. Stress can exacerbate the pain.”

(Sayori rushes to Monika’s side, gently rubbing her back while trying to keep her calm. Natsuki fetches a glass of water, and Yuri fetches a blanket to provide some warmth and comfort.)

Monika: (still in pain) “Th-thank you, everyone. I think…I th-” (feeling yet another intense wave of pain) “AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAHHHHHHHHHH!!! Oh gosh…these aren’t false alarms…”

Yuri: (frantically) “Okay, that’s it. We need to call for help now. This could be serious.”

Natsuki: (reaching into her bag) “I’m on it. I’ll call an ambulance right away- AW, DAMMIT! I forgot my phone…”

Sayori: (nervously) “I’ll use my- …oh…right…it’s at 0% battery…”

Monika: (taking deep breaths) “It’s okay, guys. I…I think we can make it to the hospital. It’s nearby.”

Yuri: “Don’t worry, we can drive there in my car.”

Study following process scheduling techniques and suggest appropriate data structures 
for their implementation along with proper justification/reason. (CLO-3, Marks 15)
A program that invoked for execution is known as a process. For execution, process enters a queue 
first (i.e. READY queue) and from this READY queue, process gets dequeued and utilize CPU for 
the execution of respective task. Process may have different attributes such as process ID, priority, 
arrival time, completion time, execution time, wait time and turn around time.
 Process ID: Unique identifier that differentiate one process from the other. e.g. p1, p2 and 
p3 etc.
 Arrival Time: Time at which the process arrives in the ready queue.
 Completion Time: Time at which process completes its execution.
 Process Execution Time: Time a process takes for executing its task.
 Turn Around Time: Time difference between completion time and arrival time
 Process Wait Time/Service Time: Time a process waits in queue due to the execution of 
other process in the queue or Time difference between turnaround time and execution time.
 Priority: Priority is associated with each process that determines the order of execution in 
priority scheduling only.
For understanding, different scheduling techniques, consider following set of processes 
along with the associated priority, arrival and execution time (provided by the user).

1. First-Come-First-Served Scheduling
As name suggests that the process coming first should be served/ or executed first. e.g. there are 
4 process P0, P1, P2 and P3. According to the arrival time (mentioned in the table 1), p0 came 
first, therefore it should be executed first for 5 sec as per its execution time. As it was the first one 
so, its service time/wait time is 0. P1 came at 1 (arrival time), so, it should be executed at second 
number for 3 sec as per the execution time provided by the user. As P1 was executed after P0 
whose execution time was 5 sec, that means P1 waited for 5sec before it execution. Similarly p3, 
will be executed at the end therefore, its waiting time should be 5+3+8=16 sec

Natsuki: (concerned) “Oh no, what happened? Is she okay?”

Yuri: “Let me see. Sky, sweetie, can you tell me where it hurts?”

Sayori: (nodding) “Yes, but they said it might take a while for them to arrive. I tried everything to calm her down, but nothing is working.”

Natsuki: “Alright, let me take a look at her. I’ve dealt with stomach pains before.”

(Natsuki carefully examines Sky, trying to identify the cause of her pain.)

Natsuki: “Sayori…you know how I’ve been reading about Allay reproductive systems? The ritual backfires due to the combination of two conflicting spells, resulting in the summoning of younger versions of Rhaenyra (Nyra) and Daemon (Daemion), who time-traveled from seventeen years ago. Ser Criston Cole, a dashing Kingsguard knight secretly in love with Nyra, is brought along with them. Faced with this unprecedented challenge, Rhaenyra must ensure the existence of her children and maintain her family’s well-being while helping her past self and Daemion return to their own timeline.

His love for Rhaenyra and desire to keep his family secure drive his pursuits.

Motivated by the desire to start a family, Daemion also seeks validation as Nyra’s prince consort and aims to prevent the Green Faction from taking power.

As tensions rise among the family, Rhaenyra and Daemon must navigate the treacherous waters of love, loyalty, duty, and responsibility for their past selves and the diverse motivations of their immediate family members. The delicate balance of their family’s future and the ultimate fate of the Targaryen dynasty hang in the balance, making every choice and alliance more critical than ever.

"Its Good to be Root

The natural tree structure of wormhole networks has significant consequences to the economic, political, and cultural evolution of the resulting societies. To get between distant parts of the network, all trade is forced to travel down its branches to the main trunk and to the root node before going up a different trunk to reach its destination. The wealth that is funneled through the root serves to enrich the influentials of the root world, through taxes, tariffs, and forcing merchants to use their services.
The nexus location of the root world makes it a natural center of administration. This is reinforced by the ability to more effectively respond to events and exert control elsewhere in the network. Where the root world was not initially a significant place of government, it often becomes one.

The central location of the root makes it attractive to businesses, as it gives them easier access to more markets. This draws not only wealth, but also talent in the form of skilled employees. These latter then demand a strong system of education, both for the benefit of the businesses and for the employee's offspring. The concentration of wealth produces disposable income that can support artists, who cater to the need for entertainment of the highly educated crowd. As a consequence, the root world becomes a place of learning, arts, and sciences.

All of these effects accrue to a lesser extent to worlds near the root node, particularly those that lie on major branch points. This encourages colonies to expand the network around them so that they themselves become a more central location and produce a lucrative flow of trade through their ports. The root world and more centralized locations also support this, as ultimately it results in more wealth passing through them as well and further enriching their citizens and power brokers.

The wealthy and influential are thus encouraged to support the status quo, and those who are not yet wealthy have the promise of one day being wealthy under the current regime. In this way, alternatives to the dominant paradigm are either neglected or actively suppressed even if they were not disadvantaged by physics itself. Proposals to create closed loops by, for example, sending the mouths of a wormhole through the wormhole network itself in order to automatically correct for the induced time lag tend to receive little support (admittedly, they would also result in either long and arduous space journeys or the need for frequent rebalancing of the wormhole links along the loop with all the resulting delays and inconveniences that would entail, while simultaneously increasing the chance of a catastrophic collapse)."

Raman spectroscopy is one of the most important inelastic scattering methods for detecting molecules and is a powerful means of providing information about their structure. Raman spectroscopy can provide valuable information in improving clinical diagnosis and therapy due to its advantages of non-invasiveness, fast response, and independence from water spectra. However, Raman still has an unavoidable drawback, in general only one photon out of millions or even hundreds of millions of photons can produce a Raman signal, i.e., the Raman scattering signal is very weak.
Multi-phonon resonance Raman scattering, as a method of Raman enhancement, is due to the effective enhancement of Raman scattering intensity when the excitation frequency is close to the electron jump frequency. As an emerging detection method, previous studies by our group have confirmed that using semiconductor nanomaterials as signal sources, this method can be used for the accurate and reliable detection of immunoglobulin G, carcinoembryonic antigen, and micro RNA.The MRRS signals are generated by resonant excitation and electro-acoustic coupling effects, whereas their isotropy arises from the vibrational energy of the photons. These intrinsic properties make MRRS immune to interference, thus ensuring its accuracy and reliability. Unfortunately, the scattering intensity is limited by the inherent electro-phonon coupling strength of the material, which hinders its further development and application.

The architecture that dots the landscape is a harmonious blend of rock and ice, exemplifying the creativity and ingenuity inherent in lomneal culture. Despite their anarchic tendencies, lomneals embrace pacifism as a way of life, rejecting violence and conflict in favor of peace and harmony. Moreover, their unwavering curiosity for the unknown and the unfamiliar manifests in their innate xenophilia, fostering a deep appreciation for other races and their respective cultures.

Summarize in up to four sentences.: Steve Martin & Martin Short Tell Conan How Their ‘SNL’ Monologue Took Three Years To Write – Watch
Peter White
Fri, August 18, 2023 at 2:23 p.m. EDT·2 min read

Steve Martin and Martin Short hosted the highest rated episode of Saturday Night Live’s Season 48 in December.

After highlighting how often they’ve each hosted – Martin 16 times and Short three times – and a bit of nostalgia when Martin showed a photo of him with John Belushi, Dan Akroyd and Mick Jagger, the pair gave each other their own eulogy.

More from Deadline

Sorry, David Bowie - The Time David Spade Turned Down A Request From The Iconic Star On 'SNL'

'SNL' Stars Please Don't Destroy Debut First-Look At Peacock Movie

Conan O'Brien Reveals What He Originally Wanted To Call 'Late Night'

“You’re not going to live forever,” Martin said. “That is sad because you won’t be able to hear the wonderful things I’m going to say at your memorial. So, I thought why wait? So, what I did was I wrote up your eulogy so you can hear it now.”

“That is such a coincidence,” added Short. “I wrote your eulogy.”

Nine months later, the pair opened up about their latest SNL experience, speaking on the Conan O’Brien Needs A Friend podcast (see video below).

The Only Murders In The Building stars had previously been on tour together – An Evening You Will Forget for the Rest of Your Life shows.

“That monologue, we worked on with the writers,” Martin told O’Brien. “We decided we were going to do a bit from our show, we’ll know we can trust it. Afterwards, it went well and the ratings were sky high, it was the highest-rated show of the year, as far as I know. I said to Marty, ‘The monologue was great, the ratings were high, so there’s a chance that we’ll be asked back to host at some other time’. Before you say yes, just remember we worked on that monologue for three years.”

The Jerk star admitted that he’d had the idea but he didn’t know how to start it. The eulogy opens with Martin saying “Wow, not much of a turnout”. “It enabled the whole piece because we could get into it with the first laugh,” he added.

O’Brien was evidently impressed. He called it “one of the funniest things”. “The moment I heard the idea, I said this is a kill-zone,” the Conan star added.

Depending on how season three of Hulu’s Only Murders In The Building, which premiered earlier this month, goes through its ten episode run, and whether SNL is able to return following the writers and actors strike, the pair may well be invited back.

synthesize this essay better and add some topic scentences "Linguicism and Its Impact on Linguistic Minority Defendants in the Criminal Justice System

Introduction

The rich tapestry of linguistic diversity is a cultural marvel that enriches social interactions and contributes to our collective wisdom. However, language barriers can hinder equal justice, particularly for linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system. Linguicism, the discrimination based on language, intersects with various aspects of the legal process and trial outcomes. This paper aims to shed light on the extent and depth of linguicism and how it affects linguistic minority defendants, guided by the hypothesis that they experience an increased likelihood of negative outcomes within the system, influenced by inadequate language support, limited access to resources, and unconscious biases of authority figures.

Review of Relevant Research

Several studies investigate the impact of language barriers and limited proficiency on the experiences of linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system. Berk-Seligson (2009) focuses on how language proficiency affects communication with legal professionals, comprehension of legal processes, and defendants’ participation in trials. Shuy (1993) examines communication barriers arising from limited language proficiency between defendants and their defense attorneys, leading to miscommunication and inadequate legal representation.

Giles and Niedzielski (1998) explore the role of biases and stereotypes related to languages or accents in determining legal outcomes, underscoring the importance of cultural competence training. Nakane (2014) emphasizes the significance of competent interpreting services for these defendants to ensure fair treatment and access to justice. Eades (2010) analyzes challenges faced by Indigenous defendants in Australia’s criminal justice system, focusing on language barriers, cultural differences, and interpreter issues. Fabela v. State of Illinois (1975) is a landmark case highlighting prejudice faced by non-English-speaking defendants due to insufficient translation and interpreting services.

Existing research explores various aspects of the issue while acknowledging the presence of communication challenges. However, differing positions arise regarding the implications and consequences of these challenges for linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system.

Data and Analysis

Drawing on empirical data and case studies, this section synthesizes information from different sources to examine the impact of linguicism on linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system.

Berk-Seligson (2009) conducted an experimental investigation into the effects of limited English proficiency (LEP) on native Spanish speakers who took part in mock trials with English-speaking police officers, attorneys, and jurors. Results revealed that police officers were more likely to perceive LEP defendants as less intelligent, persuasive, and truthful than their English-speaking counterparts. Furthermore, jurors attributed lower credibility to LEP defendants, demonstrating how language barriers can lead to prejudiced assessments of defendants’ character and reliability.

Shuy (1993) studied cases involving African American defendants who faced communication barriers due to the use of African American Vernacular English (AAVE). Misinterpretation of AAVE by White attorneys led to misunderstandings and inadequate legal representation, negatively impacting defendants’ outcomes. This suggests that linguistic differences, even within the same language, can contribute to unequal treatment within the criminal justice system.

Giles and Niedzielski (1998) found that people with nonstandard accents were more likely to be negatively evaluated by jurors, even when they were proficient in the legal system’s dominant language. Jurors’ perceptions were influenced by stereotypes associated with specific accents, leading to biased assessments of credibility and competence. This highlights how linguistic prejudice extends beyond language proficiency and may be deeply rooted in cultural and societal factors.

Nakane (2014) studied the interpreter-mediated interactions in police interviews in Japan and found that deficiencies in interpreting services contributed to discrepancies in understanding legal procedures, miscommunication, and limitations in defendants’ participation in their defense. This raises concerns about the effectiveness of current interpreting services in ensuring fair treatment for linguistic minority defendants.

Eades (2010) analyzed the experiences of Indigenous defendants in Australia’s criminal justice system, identifying significant challenges arising from language barriers and cultural differences. The study showed that communication difficulties arising from language differences and interpreter issues were exacerbated by cultural misunderstandings, racism, and stereotypes about Indigenous Australians.

Fabela v. State of Illinois (1975) is a landmark case in which an interpreter failed to provide accurate translations, leading to the conviction of a non-English-speaking defendant on charges that carried a life sentence. This case illustrates the potential for miscarriages of justice due to inadequate translation and interpreting services.

Additional Challenges Faced by Linguistic Minorities

Besides the challenges already mentioned in this paper, linguistic minority defendants also face other difficulties within the criminal justice system. For instance, they might encounter prejudice and discrimination from other participants in the legal process, such as witnesses, victims, and court staff. This discrimination can manifest as unfair treatment, biased attitudes, and social exclusion, all of which can further exacerbate their vulnerability and disadvantage within the system.

Additionally, linguistic minority defendants may also confront a lack of culturally appropriate services, resources, and support within the legal system. For example, they might not have access to legal materials and documents translated into their native language, making it harder for them to understand their rights, obligations, and the intricacies of the law. Moreover, they may also struggle to find legal representation from attorneys who are culturally competent, empathetic, and sensitive to their unique needs and circumstances.

Another significant challenge faced by linguistic minority defendants is the pervasive presence of racial and ethnic disparities within the criminal justice system. These disparities often disproportionately affect minorities, including those who face language barriers, leading to increased rates of arrest, conviction, and incarceration for this population. Consequently, linguistic minority defendants often encounter multiple layers of discrimination and marginalization within the criminal justice system, which can further exacerbate their disadvantage and hinder their access to justice.

Possible Policy and System Reforms

To counter the negative impact of linguicism on linguistic minority defendants, lawmakers and legal professionals need to adopt various strategies to ensure that the criminal justice system is more inclusive, equitable, and responsive to the needs of this population. Some possible measures include:

1. Establishing and enforcing strict guidelines and standards for interpreting and translation services within the criminal justice system, to ensure that all linguistic minority defendants have access to accurate, timely, and high-quality language support.

2. Providing specialized training and education to legal professionals, law enforcement officers, and court staff on how to work effectively with linguistic minority defendants, promote cultural understanding, and address linguistic biases and stereotypes.

3. Developing and disseminating accessible legal resources and materials in multiple languages, so that linguistic minority defendants can better comprehend the legal process and their rights within it.

4. Encouraging and supporting the recruitment of culturally and linguistically diverse attorneys, interpreters, and other legal professionals, to enhance the provision of culturally competent legal representation and services for linguistic minority defendants.

5. Implementing targeted programs and interventions to address racial and ethnic disparities within the criminal justice system, ensuring that linguistic minority defendants and other marginalized populations are treated fairly and equitably within the system.

Counterargument 

While this paper thoroughly illustrates the challenges faced by linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system and proposes several reforms to address these issues, it is essential to consider alternative perspectives on the matter. Specifically, one might argue that personal responsibility for language proficiency plays a significant role in ensuring fair treatment within the legal system.
	
Firstly, it is important to note that learning and becoming proficient in a language, especially the dominant language of the country in which one resides, is the individual’s responsibility. Lack of proficiency in the dominant language can create barriers, not only within the criminal justice system but also in various other aspects of life, such as employment, education, and social integration. Thus, it is crucial for individuals to take the initiative and learn the language to the best of their ability, which can potentially reduce the likelihood of facing linguicism and its negative consequences within the criminal justice system.

Moreover, while the criminal justice system may have measures in place to accommodate linguistic minority defendants, it can be argued that ensuring absolute fairness and equality in treatment might not always be feasible. Given the vast diversity of languages and dialects globally, the legal system might not always have the resources and personnel to provide comprehensive language support for every linguistic minority defendant. Therefore, having proficiency in the dominant language of the country would place non-native speakers in a better position to understand their rights, access necessary resources, and navigate the criminal justice system more effectively.

Furthermore, introducing comprehensive reforms to accommodate linguistic minority defendants might lead to potential delays and inefficiencies within the criminal justice system. Implementing and maintaining such measures, such as providing high-quality interpreting services and legal resources in multiple languages, could place a significant burden on the system in terms of time and resources expended. While it is necessary to ensure fairness and equal access to justice, the system needs to find a balance between addressing the needs of linguistic minority defendants and maintaining efficiency.

Therefore, while it is crucial to recognize and address the challenges faced by linguistic minority defendants within the criminal justice system, it is essential to consider the importance of personal responsibility for language proficiency as well. Achieving fluency in the dominant language of the country can be a crucial step for non-native speakers in overcoming issues of linguicism, reducing barriers to access, and ensuring fairness in their encounters with the criminal justice system.

Conclusion

This paper has examined the impact of linguicism on linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system, finding that they experience an increased likelihood of negative outcomes due to inadequate language support, limited access to resources, and unconscious biases of authority figures. The literature reveals how language barriers can lead to prejudiced assessments of defendants’ character, reliability, and competence, and how linguistic prejudice extends beyond language proficiency, rooted in cultural and societal factors.

As our world becomes increasingly interconnected and diverse, addressing this issue is crucial to ensuring that society’s quest for justice transcends linguistic barriers. Policymakers, legal professionals, and advocates for equal access to justice should consider implementing measures to improve cultural competence training, enhance interpreting services, and raise awareness about the challenges faced by linguistic minority defendants, fostering a more equitable and just legal system that transcends linguistic barriers.


Works Cited

Eades, D. (2010). The challenges for Indigenous defendants in the Australian criminal justice system. Journal of Sociolinguistics, 14(4), 538-557.

Fabela v. State of Illinois, 486 F.2d 9 (7th Cir. 1973).

Giles, H., & Niedzielski, N. A. (1998). Language attitudes: Insights from the new paradigm. Journal of Language and Social Psychology, 17(1), 101-114.

Nakane, I. (2014). Interpreter-mediated interactions in police interviews with non-Japanese-speaking suspects in Japan. International Journal of Applied Linguistics, 24(3), 361-377.

Shuy, R. W. (1993). Language Crimes: The Use and Abuse of Language Evidence in the Courtroom. Blackwell Publishers.

(As I walked to the clubroom, I couldn’t believe what happened yesterday. MC, my childhood friend, had died. The news hit me like a ton of bricks. And as I entered the clubroom, I could see Monika and Himari discussing something. Kotonhoa was also there, and was moving a strange pool - it was round and pink. Yuri was there, too - she was filling the pool with warm water. I was confused as to what they were doing, but was even more confused as to where Natsuki was.)

Yuri: “And…that should be everything!”

Monika: “Great job, Yuri. I’ll set up the decorations now.”

Himari: “I’m so excited for this pool party! It’s going to be so much fun!”

Kotonoha: “I hope everyone likes my special punch!”

(I cleared my throat to let them know I was there.)

Sayori: (trying to keep my voice steady) “Hey guys.”

(They all turned to look at me with sad expressions.)

Monika: “Sayori, how are you holding up?”

Sayori: “I’m…not doing well. MC’s death is still a really hard pill to swallow.”

Yuri: “We understand, Sayori. We’re all grieving. But we thought it might be good to distract ourselves with a pool party.”

Himari: “Yeah! We’ll have food, drinks, music, and swimming! It’ll be a blast!”

Kotonoha: “And don’t forget my special punch! It’s delicious!”

Sayori: (forcing a smile) “That sounds like a lot of fun. But…I’m not sure I’m in the mood to party.”

Monika: “We understand, Sayori. But please, try to have some fun. It’s what MC would have wanted.”

Sayori: (nodding slowly) “Yeah…you’re right. MC was always the life of the party. He wouldn’t want us moping around.”

Himari: “That’s the spirit! Now, let’s get this party started!”

Yuri: “Hold on. Does anybody else notice a lack of somebody?”

Kotonoha: “You’re right, Yuri. Where’s Natsuki?”

Monika: “I haven’t seen her all day. Has anyone tried contacting her?”

Sayori: “I texted her earlier, but she hasn’t replied.”

Himari: “I hope she’s okay.”

(Just then, Natsuki burst into the room - and immediately got into the pool, putting a hand on her massive belly. I was thoroughly confused as to why.)

Natsuki: (panting) “Sorry guys, I’m late. I just had to take care of something important.”

Monika: “Natsuki, are you okay? You look exhausted.”

Natsuki: “I’m fine. Just a little tired.”

Yuri: “And why are you in the pool already?”

Natsuki: (looking down at her belly) “Oh this? I just wanted to cool off. It’s been really hot today.”

Kotonoha: “Natsuki, are you sure you’re okay? You seem a bit…different.”

Natsuki: (smiling) “I’m fine, Kotonoha. Just a little pregnant, that’s all.”

Himari: (gasping) “Pregnant? Natsuki, that’s amazing!”

Sayori: “Congratulations, Natsuki! That’s wonderful news!”

Monika: “We’re so happy for you!”

Yuri: “Yes, congratulations.”

Kotonoha: “I can’t wait to make some special punch for the baby shower!”

Natsuki: (in severe pain) “I-I-It’s a little too late for a baby shower, Kotonhoa.”

(We all looked at Natsuki, confused.)

Himari: “What do you mean, Natsuki?”

Natsuki:“The baby’s head is halfway through.”

(We all gasped in shock and immediately sprang into action.)

Monika: “Someone call an ambulance!”

Natsuki: (gritting her teeth) “It’s too late for that. This baby’s coming out, right here…right now!”

Chapter 1: Flickering Flames

The sun was setting on the scorched plains of Hyrule, painting the land in hues of gold and crimson as it dipped below the horizon. Shadows grew longer, and with them, the twisted whispers of the Triforce of Calamity’s influence crept forth. The once-proud kingdom of Hyrule was on the verge of ruin, teetering between the eternal struggle of darkness and light.

In the heart of the Faron Woods, the secretive Yiga Clan reconvened at their hidden sanctuary. Yaqdea, the fierce Sheikah warrior and bearer of the Triforce of Power shard, entered the central chamber, her crimson eyes scanning the timeworn faces of her brethren. Tonight, they would strike out against a horde of calamity creatures that were scarring the land under the malignant sway of the Triforce of Calamity.

Master Kogah, the enigmatic leader of the Yiga Clan and Yaqdea’s adopted father, addressed the assembly. “We are the last bastions of hope in this decaying world. The time has come for us to take up arms and drive these creatures back to the shadows from whence they came.”

Yaqdea nodded, her gaze resolute. The ferocity and purpose within her had been nurtured by Kogah, and she understood the immense responsibility that had been bestowed upon her as the vessel of the Triforce of Power. She would not let her family, or the people of Hyrule, down.

As the Yiga Clan prepared to depart under the cover of darkness, the winds carried whispers of hope. Unbeknownst to Yaqdea and her comrades, Hyrule’s last beacon of light—half-Hylian, half-Zonai heir, and long-lost cousin of Aryll—was rising to meet the encroaching darkness.

Link had been raised within the sheltering embrace of the Kokiri Forest, his lineage and destiny unbeknownst to him. Awoken from a restless sleep by the Great Deku Tree Prophet, he found himself embarking on a journey that echoed his father’s tragic past—a journey to unravel his heritage and awaken his potential as Hyrule’s savior.

Clad in the humble tunic of the Kokiri, Link ventured into a world ravaged by calamity creatures and torn asunder by strife. With each step he took, he was watched from afar, his destiny drawing ever closer to that of his cousin, Princess Aryll. United by blood but separated by circumstance, the two walked divergent paths that were destined to intertwine, ultimately shaping the fate of the land they both cherished.

Through their opposing yet interconnected journeys, hope began to flicker once more across the desolate plains of Hyrule. Yaqdea faced her demons, seeking to uncover the truth about herself and the past she had left behind as Ganon’s daughter. The Triforce of Power within her pulsed with a will that threatened to swallow her whole, but something deep within Yaqdea refused to bend to its darkness.

Aryll, the last scion of the Royal Family, fought to reclaim her heritage and restore balance to her home. Her soul bound to the Triforce of Wisdom, she struggled to pierce the veil of deceit that shrouded her kingdom, risking everything to deliver her people from the darkness.

And as their paths began to converge, the fragmented souls of the Triforce bearers would form a fragile alliance, their collective strength a bulwark against the seemingly unstoppable tide of darkness.

Shadows lengthened, and the first embers of hope flickered in the darkness, harbinger of a legendary journey that would forever be etched in the annals of Hyrule—the courageous tale of three heroes bound by fate, destiny, and blood…each struggling to ensure that hope’s light would not be swallowed by the insidious shadows.


Chapter 2: Fractured Kingdom

As the last vestiges of light retreated beyond the horizon, the bitter cold of nightfall descended upon the war-torn land of Hyrule. The once-great empire lay in shambles, its vast landscape marred with scars of battles fought and lives lost. The fractures ran deep, and so too did the suffering of its inhabitants.

Link, driven by the mysterious guidance of the Great Deku Tree Prophet, could not help but feel an overwhelming sadness as he stepped out of the sanctuary of the Kokiri Forest. Gaining his bearings, he proceeded cautiously as his eyes were met with the expanse of destruction that laid before him.

Ravaged towns and villages were strewn across the landscape, their once-resilient walls now pockmarked by the relentless barrage of weapons. Fields that once nurtured bountiful crops now lay barren, the soil saturated with blood and infertile from neglect. The people who once toiled happily in these lands had either fled or perished, leaving behind only the eerie echoes of happier times.

As he traversed the desolate ruins that had once been the beating heart of the Hylian kingdom, Link glimpsed the desperation and fear that had seeped into the hearts of the people he encountered.

At every turn, the haunting specter of civil war loomed large. Across the land, the remaining Hylian feudal lords battled one another, each vying for control over the dwindling remnants of their once-prosperous domain. The brutality of their conflict had obliterated any semblance of unity and order, allowing chaos to run rampant and fester within the nation’s soul.

In the shadows of ruined castles and abandoned fortresses, desperate people clung to the whispers of a would-be savior, a long-lost princess who held within her the power of the Triforce of Wisdom. Pride and loyalty to the fallen Hyrule Royal Family stirred within these survivors, galvanizing them to rise against the usurpers who threatened to shatter the kingdom forever.

Yet, desperation often yields to blind trust, and the people, in their anguish, pinned their hopes on anyone who claimed descent from the royal lineage. Swindlers and liars abounded, using the people’s anguish to prey upon their desires for a better future. Out of hope, the people clung to these deceitful promises, only to be bitterly disappointed.

Link’s heart ached as he witnessed the suffering and strife that consumed his once-proud homeland. He observed the frail alliances born out of desperation and the festering mistrust that threatened to bring them down. It was a world that stood on the edge of utter ruin, with the last flicker of hope clinging desperately to life among the chaos.

Yet, even in the darkest recesses of the world’s despair, the indomitable spirit of Hyrule held fast. Figures cloaked in anonymity and secrecy united behind a single purpose—the resurrection of their dying land. They sought the last scion of the royal family, the missing Princess Aryll, who held the key to the Triforce of Wisdom and the last hope to restore the kingdom to its once-glorious state.

As Link ventured forth through the labyrinth of pain and destruction wrought by the clash of ambition and desperation, the fragmented souls of the Triforce bearers drew ever closer. Two unknowing cousins ventured forth, driven apart and bound together by destiny, as a warrior born of deception struggled to master the darkness within. They moved in the shadows and bided their time, preparing for the trials ahead.

Their paths would blend, lights merging against the darkness, as the shattered fragments of Hyrule’s legacy began to coalesce into a unified force, with determination and hope burning bright. As they stood at the precipice of despair, they clung to the light with unwavering courage—a beacon to the people that even in the harshest of times, hope could be found, flickering defiantly against the encroaching shadows.

Title: The Kid and Esther: Adventure Time

Opening shot shows The Kid and Esther walking through a park, carrying backpacks and water bottles. The sun is shining and the birds are chirping.

Esther: “This is going to be so much fun! I can’t wait to explore the forest.”

The Kid nods silently, his eyes scanning the surroundings.

Esther: “What do you think we’ll find out there? Maybe a hidden treasure or a secret cave.”

The Kid points to a tree, indicating that he wants to climb it. Esther laughs and follows him.

Cut to an interior shot of a dense forest. The Kid and Esther are walking, occasionally stopping to admire a tree or a bird.

Esther: “I heard that there’s a river somewhere around here. Maybe we can go for a swim.”

The Kid points to a nearby bush, then disappears behind it. Esther follows him and finds him holding a rabbit.

Esther: “Oh, it’s a cute little bunny. Can I hold it?”

The Kid hands her the rabbit, then points to a nearby pond. Esther puts the rabbit down and they both jump in the water.

Cut to a later shot of the forest. The Kid and Esther are resting on a rock, eating snacks and talking.

Esther: “You know, I’m really glad we’re friends. I always have a good time with you.”

The Kid looks at her, then reaches into his backpack and pulls out a flower. He hands it to Esther, who smiles and thanks him.

Esther: “You’re really thoughtful. I don’t know what I’d do without you.”

Suddenly, they hear a rustling in the bushes. The Kid jumps up, ready to defend himself and Esther.

Esther: “Relax, it’s just a squirrel.”

The Kid nods, then picks up his backpack and they continue walking.

Cut to a later shot of the forest. It’s getting dark, and they’ve been walking for hours.

Esther: “I think we should start heading back. It’s getting dark and we don’t want to get lost.”

The Kid nods, and they turn around. Suddenly, they hear a loud growl, and a bear appears in front of them.

Esther: “Oh no, what are we going to do?”

The Kid reaches into his backpack and pulls out a flashlight. He shines it in the bear’s eyes, causing it to back away.

Esther: “Wow, that was really brave of you. You saved us!”

The Kid nods silently, looking at the bear as it runs away.

As they make their way back home, Esther stops The Kid and kisses him on the cheek.

Esther: “Thank you for today. I had a great time.”

The Kid looks at her, then smiles shyly. They continue walking together as the sun sets over the horizon.

End of Episode 3.

Title: Online Courses for China’s External Communication: Content and Global Impact

Keywords: China, external communication, online courses, global impact, cultural exchange, international understanding

Abstract:

In recent years, there has been a surge in the demand for understanding China and its culture, in part due to its growing influence on the global stage. Various institutions have responded by developing online courses that focus on China’s external communication strategies, enabling the global audience to learn about the nation’s culture, language, and political landscape. These courses provide opportunities for increasing cultural exchange, fostering international understanding, and shaping global perspectives on China. This article seeks to explore the content of these courses, along with their impact on the world.

1. Introduction

As China’s global influence continues to expand, an increasing demand for understanding Chinese culture, language, and politics has emerged. To address this, many institutions worldwide have implemented online courses delving into various aspects of China’s external communication strategies. These courses have brought about enhanced accessibility and cultural understanding, facilitating greater engagement with China and its unique heritage.

2. Content of Online Courses for China’s External Communication

Online courses focusing on China’s external communication encompass a wide range of subjects, including:

- Chinese language and linguistics, which often focus on mastering speaking, reading, and writing skills
- Chinese history and culture, exploring topics such as Confucianism, Daoism, art, and literature
- Political landscape and international relations, allowing learners to examine China’s foreign policy objectives, economic strategies, and global presence
- Traditional and contemporary Chinese art forms, such as calligraphy, painting, Beijing opera, and cinema
- China’s socioeconomic development, covering themes such as urbanization, environmental concerns, and the country’s evolving demographics

These diverse topics allow learners with varied interests to gain insight into the complexities of China’s cultural, political, and social context.

3. Impact of Online Courses for China’s External Communication

3.1 Enhancing Global Understanding

Through offering courses aimed at increasing understanding of China, institutions foster deeper knowledge and appreciation of China’s traditions, values, and perspectives. This cultural exchange and understanding help to break down stereotypes and misconceptions about the country.

3.2 Promoting International Collaboration and Dialogue

The online courses for China’s external communication provide a platform for learners across different countries and ideologies to engage in cultural exchanges and discussions. Building connections through these interactions can lead to increased opportunities for cooperation in academia, business, and the arts.

3.3 Influencing Global Perceptions of China

By facilitating learning experiences that are both accurate and comprehensive, online courses targeting China’s external communication help to reshape global perceptions of the country. As learners develop a more nuanced understanding of China, they are likely to question and challenge prevailing stereotypes, fostering a balanced global image of China.

4. Challenges and Future Directions

While the impact of these online courses is undeniably significant, there are certain challenges to consider. Ensuring high-quality content and representation in these courses is essential to maintain their credibility and influence. Additionally, promoting real-world application and exchange opportunities, beyond the confines of online learning, is necessary to cement long-term cultural understanding.

5. Conclusion

Online courses for China’s external communication play a critical role in shaping global perspectives on the nation while fostering cultural exchange and understanding. By offering accessible, diverse, and comprehensive course materials that cater to various interests, these programs contribute to bridging international divides and promoting a greater sense of global unity. As technology advances, further innovations within online learning will pave the way for even more accessible and meaningful engagement with Chinese culture and communication strategies.

CCL5 can interact with CCR5 on the surface of NK cells, regulate the proliferation and activation of NK cells, and enhance the cytotoxicity and antiviral response of NK cells in chronic liver injury. Our results showed that CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5 were significantly increased in fibrotic livers compared with normal mice. (In the clubroom…)

Natsuki: (already fuming, despite the intense pain in her lower abdomen) “GAH! Yuri still hasn’t arrived yet?! Where is she?!”

Monika: “Calm down, Natsuki. Your anger won’t help a thing.”

Natsuki: (finally calming down) “Fine…I guess I freaked out a little too much. But…this pain is no joke. And I’m only 7 months along in my pregnancy.”

Sayori: (entering the clubroom) “What’s going on?”

Monika: “Natsuki is worried because Yuri hasn’t arrived yet, and she’s also experiencing some intense pain.”

Sayori: “Oh no, Natsuki! Are you okay? Should we call a doctor?”

Natsuki: (holding her belly) “I think I’ll be fine, but Yuri should have been here by now. I hope nothing happened to her.”

Monika: “Don’t worry, Natsuki. Let’s give her a call and check if everything is alright.”

(Sayori takes out her phone and dials Yuri’s number, anxiously waiting for her to pick up.)

Yuri: (over the phone, sounding breathless) “Hello?”

Sayori: “Yuri, where are you? Natsuki is worried, and we’re all waiting for you in the clubroom.”

Yuri: (apologetic) “I am so sorry, guys. I got caught up at the library and lost track of time. I’m on my way now.”

Sayori: “Okay, just make sure to get here safely. Natsuki isn’t feeling well either.”

(Yuri hurriedly hangs up and rushes to the clubroom.)

Natsuki: (relieved) “Finally! What took you so long?”

Yuri: (catching her breath) “I’m really sorry, Natsuki. I didn’t mean to worry you. I lost track of time while researching for a project.”

Monika: “It’s alright, Yuri. Just make sure to inform us if something like this happens again, especially when Natsuki is not feeling well.”

Natsuki: (softening her tone) “Yeah, Yuri. I know you didn’t mean to be late. Just please be more careful next time-” (her pain starts getting more intense) “OWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW!!!”

Sayori: “Natsuki, are you okay? That pain sounds really serious.”

Natsuki: (clenching her teeth, tears welling up in her eyes) “I…I don’t know…it feels worse than b-”

Monika: (interrupting) “Uh, Natsuki…? What’s that forming between your legs?”

Natsuki: (panicking) “W-What? What do you mean?!”

Sayori: “Natsuki…you might want to check.”

(Natsuki quickly checks her legs. After checking, however, her eyes widen in shock.)

Natsuki: (in disbelief) “No…it can’t be…”

Monika: (concerned) “Natsuki, what is it?”

Natsuki: (teary-eyed) “I…I think my water just broke.”

Sayori: (gasping) “Oh no! Natsuki, we need to get you to a hospital right away!”

Yuri: (nervously) “I-I can call an ambulance! Natsuki, don’t worry, everything will be okay.”

Natsuki: (trying to stay calm) “Y-Yes, call an ambulance…and someone please help me up.”

Monika: (rushing to Natsuki’s side) “I’ll help you, Natsuki. Just hold on, help is on the way.”

As Monika and Yuri help Natsuki stand up, Sayori quickly makes the call for an ambulance. The three girls wait anxiously, trying to keep Natsuki comfortable and reassured as they wait for medical help to arrive.

(Time passes…)

EMS Worker: (entering the clubroom) “We’re here for Natsuki?”

Natsuki: (gritting her teeth) “Yes, that’s me. Please, we need to go to the hospital. My baby…it’s coming-” (she suddenly starts pushing) “AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAHHH!!! Nevermind, I don’t think we have time for a hospital!”

EMS Worker: (quickly assisting Natsuki) “Alright, ma’am. We’ll do our best to handle the situation. Let’s get you to the ambulance immediately.”

Natsuki: “Did you SERIOUSLY not hear what I just said?! There’s no time for an ambulance! My baby is coming now!”

    class Status(models.TextChoices):
        ACCEPTED = 'AC', 'Accepted'
        DISQUALIFIED = 'DQ', 'Disqualified'

    code_uch_auction = models.BigIntegerField(
        validators=[
            RegexValidator(regex='^\\d{1,5}$', message='Введите число от 1 до 5 знаков', code='Invalid number')],
        verbose_name='Код участника аукциона')
    fio_predst_uch = models.CharField(max_length=250, verbose_name='Ф.И.О представителя участника')
    zagot_nasvanie = models.ForeignKey(Zagot,
                                       on_delete=models.CASCADE,
                                       related_name='far_zagot_nasvanie_uch_nasvanie',
                                       verbose_name='Название заготовителя')
    okopof_nasvanie = models.ForeignKey(Okopof,
                                        on_delete=models.CASCADE,
                                        related_name='far_okopof_uch_nasvanie', verbose_name='ОКОПОФ')
    auction_lot_number = models.ManyToManyField(NumberAuctionLot, blank=True, verbose_name='Номер аукционного лота')
    number_card_for_vote = models.ForeignKey(NumberCardForVote,
                                             on_delete=models.CASCADE,
                                             related_name='far_number_vote_card_uch_nasvanie',
                                             verbose_name='Номер карты для голосования')
    data_register_uch_auction = models.DateTimeField(verbose_name='Дата и время регистрации участника')
    status = models.CharField(max_length=2,
                              choices=Status.choices,
                              default=Status.ACCEPTED,
                              verbose_name='Статус')

    objects = models.Manager()  # менеджер, применяемый по умолчанию
    accepted = AcceptedManager()  # конкретно-прикладной менеджер
    disqualified = DisqualifiedManager()  # конкретно-прикладной менеджер

    class Meta:
        verbose_name = "Участник аукциона"
        verbose_name_plural = "4.1 Участники аукциона"
        ordering = ['-data_register_uch_auction']
        indexes = [
            models.Index(fields=['-data_register_uch_auction']),
        ]

    def __str__(self):
        return str(self.zagot_nasvanie)   в шаблоне django вывести данные из переменной auction_lot_number = models.ManyToManyField(NumberAuctionLot, blank=True, verbose_name=‘Номер аукционного лота’) имеющей тип ManyToManyField, пример кода с пояснениями
